Methods: In this pre-registered observational study, 14 widely used commercial (e.g., ChatGPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet) and open-source (e.g., Llama-4-Maverick, DeepSeek-V3) LLMs were evaluated on three official psychotherapist state exams from Germany. Questions and official solutions were independently extracted from a secure training platform restricted to registered trainees. Models produced concise final answers via standardized prompts. Two blinded raters scored all responses against the official solutions. Performance was compared with pass thresholds and human cohort averages. Sensitivity analyses focused on domain-specific performance across 10 domains (i.e., psychological, medical, pharmacological, guideline, ICD, healthcare system and legal, behavioural therapy, psychodynamic therapy, and other therapies such as systemic).
Findings: All commercial models and 7 out of 11 open-source models exceeded the pass threshold on all exams. Five models (Claude 3.5 Sonnet, ChatGPT-4o, ChatGPT-o1-preview, Llama-4-Maverick, and DeepSeek-V3) surpassed the human cohort average across all exams, in some cases by more than one standard deviation. Domain-level analyses across all LLMs showed excellent performance in medical (M=95·3%, SD=9·58) and pharmacological (M=89·1%, SD=16·10) questions but substantial deficits in psychotherapy-related domains, including psychodynamic therapy (M=54·12%, SD=22·81) and healthcare system/legal knowledge (M=62·44%, SD=21·28).
Interpretation: Several commercial and leading open-source LLMs match or exceed average trainee performance on written qualification exams, even without specialized mental-health fine-tuning. These results highlight the potential of LLMs and identify promising commercial and open-source LLMs as candidates for future clinical applications. However, further research, clear qualification standards, safety benchmarks, crisis-response safeguards, and regulatory evaluation frameworks are needed before LLM-based systems can be responsibly integrated into mental healthcare.

articles.title: Theorising the arts as a health-promoting behaviour
articles.authors: Daisy Fancourt; Robert West; Andrew Steptoe; Sharifa Abdulla; Naoki Kondo; Pier Luigi Sacco; Agustin Ibanez
articles.abstract:
Arts engagement—defined as participation in or receptivity to creative practices (e.g. music, visual art, performance, storytelling, cultural activities)—is increasingly positioned within a health behaviour framework, though this remains under-theorised. This Review advances a conceptual argument for classifying arts engagement as a health-promoting behaviour, drawing on health behaviour theory, behavioural science, and public health. It situates this within the evolution of health behaviour models, from rational-choice frameworks (e.g. Health Belief Model) to biosocial, socio-ecological, dual-process, and complex systems approaches. Arts engagement is argued to meet ontological definitions of health-related and health-promoting behaviours (e.g. Behaviour Change Intervention Ontology), as it constitutes an individual behaviour capable of improving health outcomes, intentionally or incidentally.

Two critiques are addressed: (i) that framing arts engagement as a health behaviour risks instrumentalisation, and (ii) that it implies uniformly positive effects. The paper responds by conceptualising arts engagement along a continuum from informal, intrinsically motivated everyday practices to structured therapeutic interventions, analogous to other health-promoting behaviours (e.g. physical activity). It further examines mechanisms through which arts engagement functions as a health-promoting behaviour, drawing on experimental, quasi-experimental, and epidemiological evidence demonstrating associations and causal effects across psychological, cognitive, physiological, and social outcomes. Emphasis is placed on studies using advanced causal inference methods (e.g. propensity score matching, fixed effects, g-methods, instrumental variables) and longitudinal modelling (e.g. cross-lagged panel models) to disentangle independent and bidirectional relationships while accounting for confounding and selection bias.

A roadmap for policy, intervention, and research is outlined. This includes integrating arts engagement into behavioural surveillance, applying frameworks such as COM-B, and formalising classification within behaviour change ontologies. Future research directions include cross-cultural epidemiology, ecological momentary assessment, biomarker integration, exposome-informed models, and One Health frameworks to capture multi-level, life-course dynamics.

articles.title: A Few Psychometric Properties of Work-Family Boundaries Strength Scale: Results from Three Studies in Lithuania
articles.authors: Tadas Vadvilavicius; Aurelija Stelmokienė
articles.abstract: Valid and reliable research methods are needed to assess the work-home boundaries. Data from three cross-sectional studies were used to test the psychometric properties of the Lithuanian version of Hecht & Allen (2009) Work-Family Boundaries Strength scale (internal consistency, discriminant, convergent and structural validity). Results revealed that the Work-Family Boundaries Strength subscales had high internal consistency. Adequate convergent and discriminant validity was confirmed. Finally, structural validity was confirmed by exploratory graph analysis and confirmatory factor analysis, which revealed that the two-factor structure of the scale had the best fit. The results showed that the Work-Family Boundaries Strength scale is a reliable and valid tool for assessing work-family boundaries among Lithuanians.

articles.title: The Relationship Between Parenting Styles and Children’s Problem Behavior During the Transition to Preschool: The Moderating Role of Salivary Cortisol
articles.authors: Ke Shen; Mengru Xu; Wenxin Xu; zhixinyuan; Jingmei Wang; anonymous; Jinliang Qin
articles.abstract: The transition to preschool is a critical period for children’s behavioral adaptation, during which parenting styles may play an important role. Yet children may differ in their sensitivity to caregiving environments depending on their physiological stress regulation. This study examined whether salivary cortisol moderated the associations between parenting styles and internalizing and externalizing problems during the transition to preschool. Participants were 68 Chinese children aged 2.5 to 4 years. Caregivers reported parenting styles and children’s behavioral problems, and children’s diurnal cortisol levels were derived from multi-day saliva sampling. Results showed that cortisol moderated the associations of authoritarian and authoritative parenting, but not permissive parenting, with children’s problem behaviors. Authoritarian parenting was associated with more internalizing and externalizing problems among children with higher cortisol levels. Authoritative parenting was associated with fewer internalizing problems among children with high cortisol levels but more internalizing problems among those with low cortisol levels. Permissive parenting was consistently associated with more internalizing and externalizing problems regardless of cortisol levels. These findings suggest that biological sensitivity to context during the transition to preschool is both parenting style-specific and outcome-specific. They also highlight the need to move beyond uniform recommendations about “ideal parenting” and toward more individualized parenting support that considers children’s physiological stress regulation and regulatory needs.

articles.title: Investigating the prospective associations of dietary risk factors with the risk of incident depression and anxiety in adulthood: Evidence from the UK Biobank
articles.authors: Rebecca Orr; Mojtaba Lotfaliany; Felice Jacka; Elizabeth Gamage; Melissa Lane; Emma Todd; Deb Junyi Zhang; Adrienne O’ Neil; Deborah Ashtree
articles.abstract:
Background: Mental disorders are leading contributors to disease burden worldwide. Nutritional psychiatry research supports an important role for diet in the prevention and management of mental disorders, but the relevance of specific dietary exposures requires further investigation. We examined associations of Global Burden of Disease-defined food groups and nutrients with subsequent depression and anxiety outcomes in UK Biobank participants, following the Global Burden of Disease Lifestyle And mental Disorders Taskforce protocol. 
Methods: We used data from the UK Biobank, a large biomedical cohort of more than 500,000 UK participants. Dietary intake was assessed using repeated 24-hour dietary recalls collected between 2011 and 2012 and classified according to Global Burden of Disease dietary exposure definitions. Depression and anxiety outcomes were assessed using the Patient Health Questionnaire-9 and Generalised Anxiety Disorder-7 in the 2016 Mental Wellbeing Questionnaire, after excluding participants with pre-existing depression or anxiety from the respective models. Associations were estimated using complete-case log-binomial regression models with robust standard errors, adjusting for demographic, socioeconomic, and lifestyle covariates. 
Results: Analyses included 46,503 participants in depression models and 48,656 participants in anxiety models. Overall, dietary intake was generally suboptimal, with many participants not meeting theoretical minimum risk exposure levels across several dietary factors. In fully adjusted models, higher intakes of wholegrains and fibre were associated with lower depression risk, whereas higher intakes of sugar-sweetened beverages and sodium were associated with higher depression risk. For anxiety, higher intakes of sugar-sweetened beverages, sodium and legumes were associated with higher risk, although the legume association was attenuated in post-hoc analyses excluding baked beans. Higher intakes of red meat, processed meat and trans fatty acids were associated with lower anxiety risk; however, the trans fatty acid association was attenuated in post-hoc analyses. 
Conclusions: In this large UK Biobank cohort, selected dietary exposures were associated with subsequent depression and anxiety outcomes. Findings were most consistent for wholegrains, fibre, sugar-sweetened beverages, and sodium, suggesting that specific aspects of diet quality may be relevant to later mental health. Associations for other dietary exposures were less consistent across outcomes, adjustment sets, and subgroup analyses. Further research is needed to confirm these findings and clarify potential mechanisms.

articles.title: Understanding Work–Family Enrichment Through a Cultural Lens: A Scoping Review
articles.authors: Tadas Vadvilavicius; Aurelija Stelmokienė
articles.abstract: The aim of this scoping review is to synthesize the existing research about the relationship between work-family enrichment and cultural dimensions. Four databases were used to identify relevant articles. An additional strategy of Google Scholar to search for relevant studies was performed. In all, nine articles were identified. The scoping review suggests that only three cultural dimensions are being analyzed: gender traditionalism, humane orientation, and individualism/collectivism. Furthermore, the review confirms that culture has an impact on both work-family enrichment and/or how enrichment is related to other variables in a different cultural context. Theoretical explanations of the relationships are provided. Results are discussed, and ideas for future research are proposed.

articles.title: Exploring whether the Conscioussness mediate the relation between the Presentation of False Self on SNS and Narcissism
articles.authors: Phd, Tatjana Slavko Mentus Kandić; Irena Stojković; Božidar Filipović; Marijana Veselinović
articles.abstract:
Narcissism has been consistently linked to self-regulatory processes involving impression management
and self-enhancement. Therefore we explore the prediction of false self-presentation on social network sites
through narcissism, and also when basic personality traits of BIG Five model are included as a mediators in those
relationship.
The present study examined the relationship between narcissism, as measured by the narcissism subscale of the
Dark Triad Dirty Dozen (DDTD, Dinid et al., 2018), and the presentation of false self on social networks sites (POFS
on SNS), assessed using the adapted version of the False Facebook-self questionnaire (Gil-Or et al., 2015) . As a
mediator we used consciousness measured by BFI-20 (Tucakovid, Nedeljkovid, 2025).
Using a cross-sectional design with a non-clinical youth sample (N = 1212, 54% female), mean age of M=
17.86, SD=2,64, age in range from 14 to 31, correlational and regression analyses were conducted to evaluate
whether narcissistic traits predict higher levels of POFS on SNS. Results indicated a significant positive association
between narcissism and presentation of false self on SNS, suggesting that individuals high in narcissism are more
likely to engage in self-presentation strategies that mask their authentic self. Then we explored wheather the
consciousness play moderative role in relation between narcissism and POFSon SNS. Results didn’t show any
significance. Then we explored mediative role in relation between narcissism and presentation of false self on SNS.
Direct negative effect of narcissism was confirmed (β = −.23, p < .001). Conscioussness negative effect on POFS on
SNS was confirmed (β = −.18, p < .001). Total effect of narcissism remained significant (β = .28, p < .001), and direct
effect of narcissism in predicton of POFS on SNS remained significant even after introducing the mediator
conscioussness (β = .24, p < .001). Bootstrapping analysis (5000 samples) confirmed significance of the indirect
effect of counscioussness (β = .04, 95% CI [.03, .06]), indicating the partial mediation.
These findings contribute to the understanding of narcissism as a personality trait characterized by
strategic self-construction and have implications for interpersonal functioning and psychological well-being.

articles.title: Let's be friends! People work together even when there is no incentive to do so
articles.authors: Victor Btesh; Mark K Ho
articles.abstract: One distinguishing feature of human social intelligence is our capacity to flexibly coordinate complex behaviors, such as when working together to prepare a meal. What cognitive or motivational processes make such joint action possible? Here, we investigate the role that a \emph{preference to enmesh behaviors} plays in human coordination using a novel two-player virtual baking paradigm. Participants could either independently bake bread by bringing their own water and flour, or jointly bake bread by bringing only water or only flour to share. Depending on the ingredient options available, trials in our experiment could correspond to either the classic Prisoner's Dilemma, in which acting jointly is disincentivized by the payoffs, or a "Friend's Dilemma", in which acting jointly is neither incentivized nor disincentivized (though it remains risky since one's partner might not share). Formal analysis of equilibria and dyadic learning dynamics indicate that people work together even when there is no incentive to do so. These results provide preliminary evidence that a motivation to enmesh behaviors with social partners supports successful coordination.

articles.title: Cryptic Re-perception: A Cognitive Framework for Creative-Critical Thinking
articles.authors: Andrew Veda; Luke Gerard Christie
articles.abstract:
Abstract:
Contemporary models of thinking frequently distinguish between creativity, defined as the generation of novel ideas, and critical thinking, defined as the evaluation of those ideas. While this distinction has been influential across cognitive science and education, it leaves underexamined a fundamental component of reasoning: the process by which problems themselves are interpreted and represented. This paper introduces cryptic re-perception as a distinct cognitive operation grounded explicitly in the cognitive and neural mechanisms of cryptic crossword solving, a domain that requires solvers to deliberately inhibit misleading surface readings and construct latent, non-obvious interpretations from the same linguistic material. Drawing on Ohlsson’s Representational Change Theory, predictive processing accounts of cognition, dual-process theories, research on insight problem solving, and the emerging AI literature on cryptic crossword disambiguation, the paper argues that many failures in reasoning arise not from deficits in idea generation or evaluation, but from fixation on initial interpretations shaped by prior experience. Critically, the paper addresses a gap in prior frameworks: the expansion grid proposed to operationalise structured reinterpretation is distinguished from existing heuristic tools such as SCAMPER and de Bono’s Thinking Hats by its grounding in semantic network dynamics and its derivation from attested neural mechanisms of inhibitory control and associative expansion. The paper further integrates this model with contemporary neuroscience and identifies three falsifiable predictions that distinguish cryptic re-perception from these prior frameworks and discusses an empirical research programme needed to test them. Real-world implications are examined across domains including education, decision-making, and human–artificial intelligence interaction, where the consistent failure of large language models on cryptic crossword benchmarks provides a concrete, measurable index of the reinterpretation gap the framework addresses.
Keywords: cryptic re-perception, creative-critical thinking, representational change, predictive processing, insight, inhibitory control, large language models, cryptic crosswords

articles.title: Minutes that matter: A microburst approach to adolescents’ resolution of affect in response to daily stressors
articles.authors: Paula Philippi; Aidan G.C. Wright; Aleksandra Kaurin
articles.abstract: Adolescence is a period of heightened stress vulnerability, yet the microtemporal dynamics of affective stress responses remain poorly understood. Using an ecological momentary assessment design with four 15-minute microburst-assessments within the first post-stressor hour, we tracked negative (NA) and positive affect (PA) in 288 adolescents (ages 12–21) over 14 days. Piecewise multilevel growth curve models, compared across three affective reference points (i.e., baselines), revealed sharp reactivity followed by rapid initial stabilization succeeded by decelerated recovery. Stressor intensity amplified both NA and PA reactivity and decelerated later NA recovery. Interpersonal stressors elicited stronger NA reactivity, whereas social company buffered PA decline. Adolescents with elevated internalizing symptoms showed profoundly reduced PA levels - exceeding corresponding NA elevations - alongside initially faster but subsequently decelerated PA recovery. Greater stress exposure robustly dampened affective reactivity, with effects on recovery varying by baseline specification. Results underscore the importance of temporally aligning theoretical constructs with methodological decisions.

articles.title: Exploring Predictors of Phantom Phone Signals: Phone Use, Hallucination-Proneness and Delusional Ideation
articles.authors: Emma Claire Palmer-Cooper; Lana Espina Mamode; Daniel Woollard; Haden Dewis
articles.abstract:
Background: Phantom Phone Signals (PPS) describe false perceptions that a smartphone has vibrated or made a sound, which are typically non-distressing. PPS are linked to frequent phone use, mood, anxiety, and hallucination-proneness. This study explored the role of delusional ideation, resilience, and metacognition in PPS using a novel PPS assessment.
Method: An online questionnaire study with 265 participants (mean age: 19.74; 84.2% female, no experience of psychosis) assessed PPS using the Phantom Phone Experiences and Appraisal Scale (PPEAS), along with problematic phone use (PUMP), resilience (RS-14), hallucination-proneness (MUSEQ), delusional ideation (PDI-21), and metacognitive awareness (MSAS). 
Results: PPS was highly prevalent (86.8%), generally infrequent, non-distressing and non-disruptive (mean PPEAS Impact = 4.25, SD = 3.66). PPEAS impact correlated with problematic phone use (r = .345), hallucination-proneness (r = .330), and delusional ideation (r = .288), but not resilience or metacognitive awareness. Regression indicated problematic phone use, hallucination-proneness and delusional ideation explained 18.7% of PPEAS impact variance.
Conclusion: PPS appears primarily driven by problematic phone use, hallucination-proneness and delusional ideation. Behavioural interventions may be the most effective approach for reducing PPS where required. Behavioural changes related to phone use may involve adaptive notification systems reducing perceptual errors in everyday technology use.

articles.title: Context-dependent acoustic dimensions underlying voice discrimination: Evidence from interpretable machine learning modeling of human perception
articles.authors: Tianze Xu; Xiaoming Jiang; Volker Dellwo
articles.abstract: Voice perception is often conceptualized as occurring in a multidimensional acoustic space, though the stability of its defining dimensions across contexts remains unclear. This study examined how voice discrimination varies by speaker gender and linguistic level (word vs. sentence), using behavioral data from Mandarin-speaking listeners who judged whether paired speech tokens came from the same speaker. Interpretable machine learning classifiers, both linear and tree-based, were trained on acoustic differences to predict ground-truth and perceived-identity labels. Across both label types, models performed well when trained and tested on the same linguistic level but exhibited asymmetric declines in cross-level generalization: sentence-trained models over-classified word pairs as different-speaker, while word-trained models showed the opposite bias. Feature importance analyses revealed a shared acoustic scaffold (pitch, formant structure, harmonic shape, spectral balance), alongside gender- and level-specific adaptations. Tempo cues (especially for male voices) and variability-based cues of spectral balance (especially for female voices) became more prominent in sentence-level discrimination, likely reflecting prosodic modulation and contributing to the observed asymmetry in cross-level generalization. These findings extend prototype-based models by showing that cue weighting flexibly adapts to speaker gender and linguistic level. A methodological issue in computing within-speaker variability for harmonic spectral features was also addressed.

articles.title: The CARES taxonomy: Five psychological qualities of adulthood
articles.authors: Megan Wright; Sophie von Stumm
articles.abstract: Maturity has been traditionally inferred from the attainment of socio-demographic roles, such as having a professional career, being married, and rearing children. In today’s world, these traditional ‘adult’ roles are increasingly delayed or even entirely foregone. Here, we introduce the CARES taxonomy to describe adulthood as the rich, dynamic, and rewarding phase of life that it is, independent of work, marriage, and parenting. CARES proposes that five psychosocial qualities are at the core of adult maturity: Cognitive maturity, the confidence in one’s knowledge and abilities; sense of Aging, the realisation that one is aging and that life is finite; self-Reliance, the feeling of control in life; Eudaimonia, knowing oneself and living in alignment with one’s core values and beliefs; and Social convoy, the network of relationships and level of social support in life. These five qualities have conceptual and methodological foundations in the psychological literature, map the cognitive, affective, and behavioural roots of psychosocial maturity, and form the basis of adults’ mental health and well-being. We outline how the CARES taxonomy differs from existing models of adulthood and offers a promising starting point for exploring individual differences in adult maturity.

articles.title: Six insights from climate psychology for the study of misinformation
articles.authors: Tobia Spampatti; Bojana Većkalov; Sandra Jeanette Geiger; Sander van der Linden; Stephan Lewandowsky; Matthew Hornsey
articles.abstract: Climate change and misinformation are interconnected global crises shaped by tensions between individual, collective, and vested interests. Drawing on climate psychology, which has matured under organised denial and opposition, we distill six insights for misinformation research to strengthen policy relevance, systems thinking, and resilience to attacks.

articles.title: Tracking the mind’s eyes: Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Domain-General and Domain-Specific Visual Mental Imagery
articles.authors: Chen He; Diane Lamarle; Jianghao Liu; Paolo Bartolomeo; Alfredo Spagna
articles.abstract:
Visual mental imagery (VMI) enables the generation of perceptual-like experiences in the absence of sensory input. Previous work has identified a network supporting VMI that includes frontoparietal regions, domain-preferring areas in ventral temporal cortex (VTC), and a domain-general region in left fusiform gyrus, the Fusiform Imagery Node (FIN). However, it remains unclear (i) how activity unfolds over time across these regions, (ii) what representational content they encode, and (iii) how imagery dynamics relate to visual perception.
To address these preregistered questions, we combined fMRI and magnetoencephalography (MEG) using model-based representational similarity analysis. Twenty-six participants performed imagery and perception tasks across three domains (Colors, Faces, Maps). First, imagery-related representations emerged after ~0.5 s and peaked around 1 s, substantially later than perception (~0.4 s), with no clear sequential ordering between domain-general and domain-preferring regions. Second, representational analyses revealed both domain-general and domain-specific information, with commonality analysis showing that the domain-general ROI (FIN) was predominantly driven by domain-general structure, while domain-specific contributions were temporally dissociable. Third, imagery and perception showed distinct temporal dynamics: perception exhibited an earlier, stronger, and more homogeneous pattern, whereas imagery was more sustained and temporally extended.
Together, these findings indicate that VMI relies on distributed and interactive processes rather than a simple replay of perception. Domain-specific representations emerge relatively early, while domain-general processes, including the FIN, may contribute to sustaining and integrating imagery content over time.

articles.title: Computational determinants of context-dependent preferences in probabilistic tasks.
articles.authors: Maëva L'Hôtellier; Henri Vandendriessche; Stefano Palminteri
articles.abstract: A robust feature of human (and other animals) reinforcement learning is the influence of contextual factors on individual preferences. The behavioral hallmark of this effect is that when tested outside their original setting, options that were "best among bad alternatives" are often preferred over those that were "worst among good alternatives", regardless of their true objective value. Several computational processes have been proposed to explain the origin of such relative value encoding, most notably outcome normalization mechanisms such as reference-point dependence and range adaptation (primary relative encoding). However, it has also been suggested that context-dependent behavioral signatures may emerge spuriously from other computational processes, even when values are encoded in an absolute and unbiased manner (apparent relative encoding). Among these alternative accounts, choice perseveration and asymmetric update can be argued to play a role. To resolve this ambiguity, we employed a standard reinforcement learning protocol consisting of an initial learning phase, featuring rich and poor reward environments, followed by a transfer phase to assess cross-contextual preferences. Behavioral analyses of the transfer phase revealed robust signatures of context dependence across four independent datasets. We compared four distinct classes of computational models embodying primary relative encoding and apparent relative-value processes. Model simulations established the necessity of a hybrid architecture combining a primary relative value encoding mechanism with asymmetric update in order to adequately capture human behavior. While we developed a novel range-adaptation framework suitable for probabilistic tasks, Bayesian model comparison robustly favored reference-point dependence as the mechanism best adapted to the present contingencies. Overall, this work demonstrates that multiple computational biases coexist in human reinforcement learning and that signatures of context dependence cannot be dismissed as mere artifacts.

articles.title: Comparing machine learning and conventional P300-based methods for deception detection: a systematic review
articles.authors: Vera Scheuble-Cabrera; André Beauducel
articles.abstract: Machine Learning (ML) has the potential to address highly complex problems. Deception detection is a problem for which no optimal indicator has yet been identified. However, EEG studies represent a promising approach for this task. Conventional ERP-based approaches to deception detection focus on the analysis of the P300 at a subset of electrodes, leaving much of the collected data unused. This raises the question whether ML approaches can better predict deception by building models inductively from large amounts of data. The present systematic review gives an overview of EEG studies (k=12) comparing deception detection rates between ML approaches and those based on conventional P300 analyses. Both, ML approaches and conventional P300 analyses reached accuracy rates around 80%. Although ML approaches achieved higher detection rates in most cases, the difference was small, and there was no evidence that one method systematically outperformed the other. Linear discriminant analysis and support vector machines were the most commonly used ML approaches and achieved among the highest detection rates. Neural networks were rarely used but yielded the highest detection rate. Wavelets were the most widely used preprocessing features. Studies using more electrodes did not systematically yield higher detection rates. However, in most studies, the data were highly preprocessed. It therefore remains an open question whether relevant features may be lost during preprocessing and whether the use of more electrodes would lead to higher detection rates when using data with less preprocessing.

articles.title: Attunement: a new principle of optionality
articles.authors: Benoît Leclercq; Cameron Morin
articles.abstract: Functionalist linguists generally maintain that language favours exclusive form-meaning mappings, adhering to principles of isomorphism or no synonymy. Variationist linguists, by contrast, emphasise that language is rife with alternative ways of saying the same thing, aligning with a principle of optionality. This theoretical piece argues that the apparent tension between these perspectives can be resolved by attending to two sets of background assumptions: what counts as meaning, and how communication works. We contend that a usage-based theory of meaning, encompassing semantic, pragmatic, and social information, is not only compatible with variation but entails it; and that a cognitive theory of communication, informed by good-enough production and relevance-theoretic heuristics, explains why optionality phenomena emerge in language use without compromising isomorphism in linguistic knowledge. We subsume these insights under a newly proposed Principle of Attunement. We explore the explanatory scope of attunement for register-driven variation, the expendability cline, creative language use, and language change, before introducing the contributions to the special issue.

articles.title: The Mind's Compass: Semantic control as the guiding mechanism of affordance management
articles.authors: Maria Montefinese; Irene Di Pietro; Giada Viviani; Ettore Ambrosini
articles.abstract: Ko and Neuberg's (2025) three-pillar framework for affordance management lacks a central guidance mechanism. We argue that their pillars (perception, behavior, and environment) are directed by semantic control, the cognitive ability to flexibly manage conceptual knowledge to suit goals. This neurocognitive system provides the "how" behind adaptation, and its own developmental trajectory constrains the management of life-stage challenges.

articles.title: Public positivity and private prudence: Differences in prescribed optimism for thoughts and appearances
articles.authors: Erik Løhre; Subramanya Prasad Chandrashekar; Asude Levent
articles.abstract: Research on prescribed optimism has found that people generally believe others should be optimistic rather than realistic or pessimistic. We argue that previous studies on prescriptions have overlooked the social context, i.e., whether a future outlook is thought about privately or expressed publicly, with public contexts evoking self-presentation concerns that could increase the value of optimism. Four pre-registered experiments (total N = 1249) show that people think others should be more optimistic in public than in private. In Experiment 1 and 2, using hypothetical scenarios, participants prescribed more optimism for public appearances than for private thoughts in both within- and between-subjects comparisons. Experiment 3a and 3b investigated prescriptions for positive and negative events related to two current high-importance topics, climate change and AI. Again, results pointed towards more optimistic prescriptions for public expressions than for private thoughts, and additionally showed more optimistic prescriptions for a highly involved role (a CEO) than for less involved roles (scientist or average citizen). The findings suggest that people do not endorse biased thinking about the future as much as they endorse putting on a positive face. These social and reputational concerns may have implications for communication practices of leaders and officials, and could contribute to distorted, overoptimistic perceptions of the future among the public.

articles.title: Construction and Validation of the Deliberate Ignorance Scale (DIS)
articles.authors: Raymond Godwin; Unggul Wiryawan Siswanto; Bonanza Octyokora Fayaseaco
articles.abstract: While humans are often considered to have a fundamental drive to pursue knowledge, they frequently choose to remain ignorant even when it is important and easy for them to know. Termed as Deliberate Ignorance (DI), this paradoxical decision differs from another avoidance behaviors like willful ignorance (WI) and information avoidance (IA), which are more defensive responses. This research constructed and validated the Deliberate Ignorance Scale (DIS) using a formative composite scoring method to capture this construct through an exploratory study in a health context (Study 1, N = 322) and a confirmatory study in a relationship context (Study 2, N = 162). Factor analyses established a robust three-factor structure: Perceived Importance of Knowing the Information (PIKI), Perceived Ease of Accessing the Information (PEAI), and Tendency to Ignore the Information (TII). Results also showed that the perceived importance of knowing remains unaffected by perceived threat. This independence signifies DI as a strategic decision, not a mere defensive response like IA. The DIS captures this structural paradox of Homo ignorans by positioning individual along a continuum from active information seeking to deliberate ignorance under conditions of acknowledged importance of knowing and accessibility.

articles.title: Can we distinguish briefly-presented natural scenes equated in summary statistics and semantic meaning?
articles.authors: Liang Qianchen; Makiko Yamada; Yasunori Aizawa; Naotsugu Tsuchiya
articles.abstract: Our visual experience feels rich, yet early studies suggested that what we can report about the experience is limited to 3~4 items at a glance. To reconcile this discrepancy, some studies argued that our momentary experience is a compressed summary of the visual inputs, which allows us to understand the general semantic meaning (gist) of the scene, creating an impression of richness despite lacking in details. Conversely, others proposed that our experience is perceptually rich and specific, but we have a limited ability to access and describe its details in words. To distinguish between these possibilities, we invented a novel psychophysics paradigm to examine whether participants can discriminate between images that are equated both on semantic gists and summary statistics. For each of 100 natural scenes, we synthesised an image with matched summary statistics. In Experiment 1 (N = 100, completed), we collected gist descriptions for each image, and evaluated whether each image pair is semantically-equivalent. Using these image pairs equated in both gists and summary statistics, in a series of registered Experiments, we will perform a perceptual discrimination task on online participants and in-lab participants with immersive displays. Overall, our experiments will investigate whether our experience contains perceptual details that cannot be described in words.

articles.title: From the Lab to the Classroom: Bridging Psychophysics and Psychometrics to Improve Adaptive Dyslexia Screening
articles.authors: Wanjing Anya Ma; Benjamin Domingue; Guilherme Lichand; Karine Votikoske Roncete; Lucas Iantorno Klotz; Carrie Townley-Flores; Adam Richie-Halford; Jason D. Yeatman
articles.abstract: As children learn to read, two skills develop in parallel: the complexity of words they can decode and the speed at which the word recognition system operates. Reading assessments capture these processes using both timed and untimed tasks, yet it remains unclear whether word processing speed and word complexity reflect separable dimensions of reading ability or two manifestations of the same underlying skill. Here, we independently manipulated word complexity and presentation time in a lexical decision task to test whether they constitute distinct axes of reading development. Through a research–practice partnership, we collected large-scale developmental data from 3,194 students spanning Grades 1–12 in the United States (English) and Brazil (Portuguese). Results showed that items presented with infinite presentation time were systematically less difficult but required longer response times than items presented under fast conditions for students with and without dyslexia across development. Importantly, word processing speed and word difficulty were well characterized by a unidimensional framework, suggesting that the increasing speed and complexity observed during reading development reflect a common underlying skill. We then leveraged these findings to design and validate a novel multi-stage adaptive-timing approach for dyslexia screening (N=14,393 calibration sample; N=841 validation sample). Results from a K–2 validation study showed that this approach improved screening precision and efficiency for beginning readers. By integrating the experimental precision of Psychophysics with scalable measurement frameworks in Psychometrics, this work illustrates how insights from basic science can be translated into universal school-based screening practices for early dyslexia identification.

articles.title: Investigating the Perceived Unpredictability of Artificial Intelligence: The Role of Temperature and Pre-Prompting
articles.authors: Oliver Lack; William Xiang Quan Ngiam; Carolyn Semmler
articles.abstract: With the growing prevalence of systems that use generative models, understanding how humans perceive these systems is of utmost importance. AI systems are widely characterised as “unpredictable,” potentially eroding user trust, safety, and reliable adoption. Developers routinely tune the temperature hyperparameter of foundation models to manage this risk. It is unclear how the temperature hyperparameter and pre-prompt influence the unpredictability of Foundation Models (FM). This study examined human-AI interactions within a novel experimental context. 141 participants were randomly assigned to varying temperature or pre-prompted conditions across OpenAI and Anthropic models to complete a Wordgame Task using a generalisable AI interface. Results revealed that temperature robustly modulates objective metrics related to unpredictability, such as perplexity and information entropy of output. Contrary to pre-registered hypotheses, human-rated unpredictability was not influenced by temperature. However, manipulating pre-prompt settings yielded strong, convergent relationships between both human-rated unpredictability and other unpredictability metrics across models. Post-hoc generation of FM unpredictability ratings captured statistical regularities with human ratings but revealed more nuanced mapping to the objective predictability of interaction output. Overall, backend prompt engineering is an effective method for shaping human perceptions of AI predictability, not temperature.

articles.title: Automated Temporal Speech Markers of Childhood Stuttering
articles.authors: Vishruta Yawatkar; Ho Ming Chow; Emily Garnett; Soo-Eun Chang; Evan Richard Usler
articles.abstract: A major challenge in speech-language pathology has been accurately and reliably quantifying speech (dis)fluency, particularly the perceptible speech disruptions associated with childhood stuttering. We used a custom Automatic Temporal Analysis of Speech (ATAS) pipeline to detect and measure pause and vocal events during oral reading in 60 school-age children (6–13 years old) who do (CWS, n = 30) and do not stutter (CWNS, n = 30). ATAS metrics include speech rate, total pause time, pause count, mean pause duration, mean vocal duration, and variability in pause and vocal duration. Our results largely supported our hypothesis that CWS exhibit a slower and more fragmented speech flow during oral reading than CWNS. This pattern manifests as a slower speech rate, shorter mean vocal duration, longer total pause time, and more pause events. Many of these temporal metrics are also associated with traditional measures of stuttering frequency and severity. Furthermore, increasing age was associated with faster speech rate, fewer pauses, and reduced event duration variability, particularly in CWNS. Machine-learning analyses across multiple classifiers and feature configurations showed that ATAS-derived temporal features improved individual-level detection of childhood stuttering beyond demographic and chance baselines. The strongest performance was achieved by a random-forest classifier trained on age and ATAS metrics, yielding accuracies up to 92% across folds (mean ≈ 69%) under nested cross-validation. Together, these findings provide proof-of-concept support for rapid automated temporal measurement of speech production as an alternative and complementary tool for the quantification of overt speech disfluency in CWS.

articles.title: Brain connectivity-driven digital twins of decision-making for personalized psychiatric intervention
articles.authors: Yuta Takahashi; Ryusei Murata; Yuichi Yamashita; Shingo Murata
articles.abstract: Precision psychiatry requires digital twin models that link brain characteristics directly to decision-making. Here, we present NeuroPolicy, a hypernetwork-based framework that transforms resting-state functional connectivity (rsFCM) into participant-specific parameters of a recurrent neural network, thereby enabling individualized trial-by-trial decision-making dynamics to be generated directly from brain connectivity. In simulations, NeuroPolicy recovered ground-truth computational structure with strong correspondence to the latent representation (r = 0.88), while achieving high prediction accuracy (0.758–0.774). Applied to the NSPN cohort (n = 274), NeuroPolicy achieved approximately 0.72–0.73 accuracy in cross-timepoint and zero-shot inter-subject prediction from rsFCM-derived individualized models. Crucially, its differentiable mapping enabled in silico intervention optimization, improving optimal-action rates in 78.2% of individuals (mean improvement of 0.115, Cohen’s d = 0.99) and identifying personalized intervention directions involving prefrontal and frontoparietal connectivity. NeuroPolicy provides a framework for zero-shot behavioral prediction and biologically grounded intervention simulation in precision psychiatry.

articles.title: Purpose and agency in evolutionary biology and psychology
articles.authors: Frank Masterpasqua
articles.abstract:
Abstract
For more than 100 years, the prevailing perspective on evolution has been gene centric.  It is a perspective from which random genetic mutations that happen to lead to naturally selected adaptive phenotypes are seen as the causes of evolution.  According to this “Modern Synthesis”, “selfish genes” are insulated from any influences of organisms or their environments, and function autonomously.  This article presents recent molecular and biological research that has led to an "Extended Evolutionary Synthesis” in which an organism’s development and purposive agency are the drivers of evolution. In addition to the influence of development on evolution, the focus moves to understanding how the relations between evolution and psychology change when evolutionary biologists describe humans, and all organisms, as purposive agents in evolutionary change. The Extended Synthesis has far-reaching implications for psychology, including the possible roles that psychological factors can play in shaping an evolutionary future. 
Keywords: evolution, purpose, agency

articles.title: Beyond Dissemination: From Critical Science Literacy to Citizen Neuroscience
articles.authors: Nora Maria Raschle; Elena Federici; Tobias U. Hauser; Martin Dresler
articles.abstract: Neuroscience increasingly informs education, mental health, and public policy, yet its real-world impact remains limited, with misunderstandings and neuromyths persisting. We argue that the societal impact of neuroscience communication depends less on the volume of information disseminated than on whether it cultivates critical science literacy; namely, the ability to evaluate how neuroscientific knowledge is generated, modeled, and constrained, and to understand the inferential steps linking data to conclusions and their limits of generalization. In the context of neuroscience, we refer to this as critical neuroscience literacy to emphasize the domain-specific challenges of interpreting brain-based evidence. The primary barrier is not insuEicient dissemination alone, but systematic misinterpretation of neuroscientific inference once findings leave the laboratory. We advance three claims: first, that public misunderstandings often reflect limited insight into scientific reasoning and practice rather than simple information deficits about scientific results; second, that citizen science and dialogical engagement function as mechanisms for practicing such reasoning rather than as outreach add-ons; and third, that neuroscientists share responsibility for shaping the interpretive conditions under which findings are translated, particularly in education, health, and policy. Importantly, this self-referentiality is not merely descriptive but can be causally consequential, as interpretations influence beliefs, expectations, and environments that in turn shape neural systems. We outline implications for training, incentives, and professional norms within the neuroscience community.

articles.title: The influence of glucose intake and psychosocial stress on food craving in healthy adults
articles.authors: Flora Nasilowski; Julia Reichenberger; Bernadette Denk; Raphaela Gaertner; Elea S. C. Klink; Eva Unternaehrer; Nina Volkmer; Stella Wienhold; Jens C Pruessner; Maria Meier
articles.abstract:
Stress has been linked to the consumption of palatable food and may contribute to the development of obesity. Interindividual differences in cortisol stress reactivity are discussed as mediating mechanisms, yet findings linking food craving to cortisol reactivity are heterogeneous. Methodological differences in whether participants receive a sugary preload or are invited fasted may contribute to these divergences. Hence, this study set out to systematically investigate the effect of glucose and psychosocial stress on food craving in healthy adults. 
Overall, 62 healthy, fasted adults (mean age = 23.13; SD = 3.02; 54.84 % female, 45.16 % male) reported current food craving on the Food Craving Questionnaire, received either glucose or water and were later exposed to psychosocial stress or a friendly control condition. After stressor exposure, food craving was assessed a second time. We repeatedly assessed salivary cortisol and blood glucose and used linear mixed models to test whether the experimental manipulations predicted changes in craving and explored associations with sex and stress-eating tendency.
Blood glucose increased in those consuming glucose and the stress test increased cortisol significantly. Higher cortisol reactivity, and even stronger, a greater increase in blood glucose led to significant reductions in food craving over time. We found no supportive evidence that glucose and cortisol reactivity interactively affected craving. 
These findings suggest that short-term metabolic signals may be more influential in shaping immediate food craving than acute stress, and that acute cortisol elevations may not universally promote food craving.

articles.title: Dysphagia and Dysphonia in Parkinson’s Disease: Pathophysiological Mechanisms, Treatment Approaches, and Clinical Implications
articles.authors: Elizabeth Hong
articles.abstract: Parkinson’s Disease (PD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by the degeneration of dopamine-producing neurons, leading to a wide range of symptoms that significantly impact patients’ quality of life. Among these, dysphagia (difficulty swallowing) and dysphonia (voice impairments) are particularly prevalent, affecting a majority of individuals with PD and contributing to serious health risks and social challenges. Dysphagia affects up to 80% of individuals with PD, posing risks such as aspiration pneumonia, malnutrition, and dehydration. These clinical manifestations can lead to increased morbidity and mortality, making early detection and intervention essential. Current treatment approaches for dysphagia include compensatory strategies, such as posture adjustments, swallowing exercises, and dietary modifications, as well as pharmacological interventions. However, given the progressive nature of PD, individualized treatment plans are necessary to effectively manage symptoms and prevent life-threatening clinical manifestations. Similarly, dysphonia affects approximately 70-90% of PD patients and results in vocal impairments, including reduced volume, breathy or hoarse speech, and monotone voice, which hinder communication and contribute to social isolation. The loss of vocal strength and clarity can lead to frustration, withdrawal from conversations, and diminished overall well-being. The Lee Silverman Voice Treatment (LSVT) has emerged as one of the most effective therapeutic interventions for dysphonia, aiming to improve vocal loudness and clarity. While LSVT has shown promising results, research on dysphonia remains less extensive than dysphagia, indicating a need for further investigation into optimized treatment strategies. Given the profound impact of both dysphagia and dysphonia, a multidisciplinary approach integrating speech-language therapy, medical interventions, and tailored rehabilitation programs is crucial. Addressing these symptoms proactively can help improve communication, reduce health risks, and ultimately enhance the overall quality of life for individuals living with Parkinson’s Disease.

articles.title: Replicating insight, insights from replication: Methodological reform in creativity research
articles.authors: Radwa Khalil; Sergio Agnoli; Arvid Kappas; Wendy Ross
articles.abstract: Over the past 75 years, psychological science has investigated creativity using established methods. These robust approaches have examined how individuals generate novel thoughts. Such rigor has helped the field shift toward open science, which values robust and reproducible results over novelty alone. Open materials, data, code, preregistration, registered reports, and replication all foster advancement. In 2014, the American Psychological Association (APA) published a special issue that called for greater emphasis on replication in creativity research and promoted stronger initiatives. Despite these efforts, creativity research still lacks a strong culture of replication. This issue likely stems from the fact that creativity research spans many domains and methods. To address this problem, researchers and institutions should prioritize reporting and critically discussing unsuccessful replications in insight problem-solving experiments. Researchers must champion transparent replication and preregistration, and journals should commit to publishing well-conducted failed replications. These steps will drive scientific progress. Robust replication challenges existing paradigms and fosters creativity by confirming findings or revealing new directions.

articles.title: A Multimethod Study of Speech Perception and Language Experience in Yucatec Maya–Spanish Bilinguals
articles.authors: Christine Shea; E. Haydée Carrasco-Ortíz
articles.abstract: This study examines speech perception, language use, and language ideology among university-educated Yucatec Maya–Spanish bilinguals in Yucatán, Mexico. Building on recent work, we conceptualize perception as gradient and shaped by linguistic experience. We adopt a multimethod approach integrating (a) Visual Analog Scaling (VAS) tasks assessing perception of tone (Maya), vowel duration (Maya), and voice onset time (VOT; Spanish); (b) egocentric social network measures of language use; and (c) qualitative analyses of language attitudes and identity. Results show systematic differences across continua: tone elicited more gradient responses, whereas duration and VOT showed more categorical patterns. Gradiency was significantly associated with language experience, including Maya dominance and the proportion of Maya-speaking interlocutors, while consistency remained stable. Qualitative findings indicate that participants view Maya as central to identity but navigate institutional pressures that privilege Spanish. These findings support a view of speech perception as shaped by socially structured linguistic experience.

articles.title: Restricted Environmental Stimulation and Psychopathology
articles.authors: Marek Malůš; Martin Kupka; Daniel Dostál; Veronika Kavková
articles.abstract: The aim of the study was to assess the possible influence of the overall context of a modified version of Chamber REST (Darkness Therapy - „DT“) on the psychopathological induction. The research sample consisted of 37 clients of the Beskydy Rehabilitation Centre in Čeladná who willingly underwent a week of restricted environmental stimulation - DT (a version of Chamber REST), during 2012 – 2013. The age ranged between 25 and 84 (M = 45.86; SD = 13.16). The sample consisted of 17 women and 20 men. The participants were selected by purposive voluntary sampling. A week before the DT stay, each of the subjects filled in the Symptom Checklist 90 (SCL-90). Three weeks after they completed the procedure they received SCL-90 again. The data obtained were evaluated by the SPSS software. When comparing the values measured before and after the stay in the researched modification of Chamber REST (Dark Therapy) environment, we found a significant difference on all scales and on a total score, too.

articles.title: No Evidence of Word Segmentation in Monodialectal or Bidialectal 9-Month-Old Norwegian Infants
articles.authors: Ana Boskovic; Audun Rosslund; Julien Mayor; Natalia Kartushina
articles.abstract: One of the earliest challenges in language acquisition is segmenting words from continuous speech. One mechanism supporting this process is statistical learning – the ability to track distributional regularities in input (Saffran et al., 1996). Although this mechanism is well established in monolingual infants, its role under within-language variability (e.g., dialect exposure) remains unclear. Norwegian is particularly relevant in this context, as it lacks a standardized spoken form and consists of a continuum of regional dialects differing in phonology, prosody, and grammar. This study examined whether 9-month-old Norwegian infants segment speech using transitional probabilities and whether bidialectal exposure influences performance. In a conceptual replication of Saffran et al. (1996), 91 infants (55 monodialectal and 36 bidialectal) were familiarized with an artificial speech stream containing four trisyllabic words defined by transitional probabilities. Segmentation was indexed by differences in looking times to familiar versus novel items using eye-tracking. Bayesian analyses indicated moderate evidence for the null hypothesis in both monodialectal (BF = 0.20) and bidialectal (BF = 0.18) infants, as well as for the lack of group difference (BF = 0.25). These findings provide no evidence for transitional-probability-based word segmentation in Norwegian infants at 9 months, regardless of dialectal exposure, pointing to potential cross-linguistic differences in the onset of early statistical learning.

articles.title: “I just feel like every day I’m missing out”:  Young people’s experiences of living with Long Covid
articles.authors: Ayesha Hafiz; Simon Brett; Josie Briscoe; Eden Byrne; Conor Flaherty; Sanjana Jaidka; Eva Peters; Sue Peters; Katie Robinson; Kirsty Stanley
articles.abstract: Background. Long Covid is a multi-system condition that profoundly disrupts people’s everyday lives. While research on adults’ experiences is growing, little is known about its impact on young people. This study sought to address this gap, gaining an in-depth account of the experiences of young people with Long Covid. Methods. We adopted a participatory qualitative design, combining expertise from young people with Long Covid, and adults with experience of Long Covid and academic research, as co-researchers throughout the study. We conducted individual semi-structured interviews with 13 young people, aged 13 – 20 years (M = 16.6, SD = 2.1) between August 2024 and May 2025. Interviews focused on phenomenological experiences, education, social life, healthcare experiences, perceived impact of Long Covid and future hopes. We analysed the data using reflexive thematic analysis within an essentialist framework. Results. We identified four themes: (1)“It’s kind of been relentless”, describing the sudden onset of debilitating symptoms, pervasive fatigue, and invalidation by healthcare professionals; (2)“It really kind of takes away your identity”, highlighting profound disruptions to education, hobbies and social relationships – and to young people’s emerging sense of self; (3)“The supports just aren’t there”, reflecting systemic failures in healthcare and education, alongside stigma and inequities; and (4)“The uncertainty is quite difficult to live with”, capturing the mental toll of the associated unpredictability and isolation. Conclusions. Our findings demonstrate that Long Covid exerts far-reaching effects on young people’s physical health, identity, education and mental wellbeing, compounded by systemic shortcomings and stigma. Addressing these challenges requires coordinated action across healthcare, education and policy domains, with inclusive, holistic supports co-designed with young people themselves.

articles.title: A Megastudy of Behavioral Interventions to Increase Voter Registration Ahead of the 2024 U.S. Presidential Election
articles.authors: Katherine Anne Mason; Kayley Okst; Don Green; Robb Willer; James Druckman; Jan G. Voelkel; Yash Patel; Claire Robertson; Wooyoung Choi; Jasmine Sanchez
articles.abstract: In the United States, in nearly all cases, one must register in order to vote—yet, a substantial portion of the eligible electorate remains unregistered. Despite this, relatively little is known about how to increase the likelihood that a voter registers. Here, we tested the impact of 10 expert-crowdsourced, theoretically-based psychological interventions on a sample of eligible, yet unregistered, U.S. voters ahead of the 2024 presidential election (N = 12,896). Eight of the interventions increased intentions to vote, and five led individuals to click on the voter registration website. Escalating Commitment, which sequentially employed several social pressure strategies, was the strongest intervention across these outcomes. However, none of the interventions had a significant effect on actual voter registration or voter turnout. The results highlight a substantial disconnect between voters’ intentions and their ultimate behaviors. We discuss potential structural and psychological barriers that undermine the translation of intent into action.

articles.title: Modeling the Vigilance Decrement in a Two-Boundary Signal Detection Task
articles.authors: Fernando Muñoz Gómez Andrade; Kayla Rose Hankey; Yusuke Yamani; Jason S. McCarley
articles.abstract: Objective: We fit a generative model to data from a sensory monitoring task to isolate mechanisms of the vigilance decrement. Background: Although the vigilance decrement has been studied for decades, analytic constraints have impeded a theoretical agreement on the causes of the effect. Generative models offer a technique to disentangle sources of vigilance failure that are conflated by traditional performance measures. Method: Participants performed a signal detection task that required them to judge whether the position of a visual probe each trial was sampled from a narrow or a wide probability distribution, where both distributions shared the same midpoint. Data were fit with a generative cognitive model that assumed the observers could make each judgment in either an attentive or inattentive state. Attentive observers made judgments by comparing the estimated probe position to a pair of decision cutoffs on either side of the display midpoint. Inattentive observers guessed their responses. Parameters corresponding to sensitivity, response bias, attentional lapse rate, and positive guessing rate were allowed to vary across blocks of trials. Results: Target detection rates dropped over time on task, showing a clear vigilance decrement. Model fits ascribed the effect to conservative bias shifts, attention lapses, and a change in response guessing strategy. Conclusion: Generative cognitive models offer a technique to better understand the mechanisms and test theories of the vigilance decrement. Application: Theory-based computational models can improve efforts to predict and mitigate vigilance failures.

articles.title: Consumo de alcohol en adolescentes de 10 a 20 años de edad y su relación con los factores familiares en el contexto latinoamericano desde una revisión sistemática de literatura entre los años 2015 y 2020
articles.authors: Carlos Fernando Pantoja-Muñoz
articles.abstract:
The   following   systematic   review   aimed   to   analysethe   relationship   between   alcohol consumption and family factors in adolescents aged 10-20 years in the Latin American context between 2015 and 2020, by researching scientific articles in virtual search platforms such as: Redalyc, Scielo, and Google Scholar. Method: qualitative, applied through a systematic review, whose purpose is to make use of criticism in an orderly, precise and analytical manner of the topic of interest while pointing out the similarities and inconsistencies in the literature analysed (Guirao,   2015).   Results:   It   was   possible   to   explain   the   relationship   between   alcohol consumption in adolescents and its relationship with family factors; typifying them according to their triggers, including family functionality, family communication, family cohesion, family perception, family permissiveness, family violence and resilience, internet management, social skills,  sexual  aggression,  gender  violence  and  sex  and  age.  Conclusions:  It  was  possible  to identify  that  alcohol  consumption  in  adolescents is  related  to  family  functionality  due  to  the repetition of behavioural patterns caused by a family member and the relational dysfunctions experienced  in  the  different  stages  of  the  family  life  cycle.  Thus,  it  can  be  said  that  alcohol consumption is directly related to the family (Parra, 2020).

Keywords:adolescents, alcohol consumption, family factors

articles.title: Boys and Things, Girls and People?  Gender-Related Interest Patterns in Elementary School
articles.authors: Benjamin Goecke; Jessika Golle; Ingo Zettler; Ulrich Schroeders
articles.abstract: Gender-related interest patterns are well documented in adolescence and adulthood, but little is known about whether comparable patterns can already be identified in elementary school, whether they extend beyond subject-specific academic interests, and whether they are organized along the same broad dimensions as in older samples. In this study, we developed an age-appropriate self-report measure of RIASEC-based activity preferences for elementary school children. In a sample of more than 1,000 third graders, interests were assessed at the beginning and end of one school term. Items were constructed to represent the six RIASEC domains and selected for the final instrument using Ant Colony Optimization. We examined the measure’s psychometric properties, including structural validity, as well as gender differences, short-term temporal stability, and associations with academic interests and school grades. Results revealed a RIASEC-like circumplex structure in this young age group for both girls and boys. At the beginning of Grade 3, children showed distinct gender-related interest profiles consistent with patterns reported in older populations, with boys showing stronger things-oriented profiles and girls showing stronger people- and ideas-oriented profiles. These gender differences remained essentially stable across the term, and vocational-like interests showed small-to-moderate, theoretically plausible associations with subject-specific academic interests and school grades. Overall, the findings suggest that gender-typed organization of broad activity preferences is already in place in middle childhood, well before children make consequential educational choices, and that interest assessment beyond curricular labels can capture motivational structures not visible in subject-specific measures.

articles.title: DECISIONS, VARIABILITY, AND VISUALIZATION: A NOVEL INSTRUMENT FOR DECISION MAKING UNDER VARIABILITY
articles.authors: Zachary del Rosario; Erika Saur; Jin; Jessica Lin; Jeremy Bennet Wilmer
articles.abstract: To make statistically sound choices, decision makers must respond to the consequences of variability—to target variability. Previous work has shown that targeting is beneficial, but there are questions about its prevalence and contributing factors. With a novel instrument and a demographically representative, randomly assigned n=306 sample, we find that most U.S. adults target variability by default, even with minimal information (95% CI [78%, 92%]). Surprisingly, the same information presented via a standard bar graph inhibits targeting, while showing raw data enhances targeting. These results support a resource view of statistically sound decision making (over a deficit view), and contradict widespread ideas in data visualization (that raw data will overwhelm).

articles.title: DINÁMICAS RELACIONALES DE FAMILIAS DIVERSAS: UNA MIRADA A LA  FAMILIA DE ELECCIÓN EN PERSONAS TRANS
articles.authors: Carlos Fernando Pantoja-Muñoz; Aura María Pérez Beltrán; Laura Rosalba Velandia Viracacha
articles.abstract:
This study interprets the relational dynamics in trans families of choice through narrative discourse, exploring how power discourses are related in their interactions from a clinical systemic constructionist perspective. A qualitative perspective is adopted, specifically the narrative method and the instrument applied was the artistic devices. The objective was to understand how power discourses influence the construction and negotiation of roles, identities and family relationships in the context of trans families of choice. And the guiding research question was How do discourses of power influence the construction and negotiation of roles, identities and family relationships in the context of trans families of choice, in order to categorize these discourses and their impact. The population is composed of two families in Bogotá. The results show the forms of desubjectivation in the participants as a critical way of constructing social and community versions of what it means to be a trans woman. What was found is located in the resignification of life experiences that sought to understand the impact of the construction of the self in the discourse and thus resist disassociating and patriarchal forms of discourse. 
Keywords: trans families of choice, power discourses, family configurations, gender 
violence, narrative methods, artistic dispositifs.

articles.title: Bounded Morality: The Psychophysics of Morality
articles.authors: Ansgar D Endress; Peter Ayton
articles.abstract: Moral decisions are often cast as a conflict between utilitarianism (maximizing good) and deontology (following rules). We propose a unitary psychophysical model where moral decisions operate like other perceptual systems and track the subjective (information-theoretic) certainty that outcomes achieve the greater good. Due to psychophysical noise, decisions are governed by Weber’s law, with utilitarian and deontological responses as high- vs. low- uncertainty limit cases. Participants rated the acceptability of sacrificing fewer ‘victims’ to save more ‘beneficiaries.’ Consistent with this model, acceptability tracked the ratio of beneficiaries to victims (beneficiary:victim Weber ratios), but not utility (net survivors) or harm (victims). With decreased uncertainty (using decisions concerning economic goods that had clearer values than human lives), responses became more ‘utilitarian’; with increased attention to victims (via emotional manipulations), responses became more ‘deontological’, but always tracked the beneficiary:victim Weber ratios. Frontier large language models from three different jurisdictions (though not smaller models) partially reproduced human behavior, but used qualitatively different heuristics from humans and generally prioritized harm or utility over beneficiary:victim ratios. Beyond moral decision making, the same model provides the first mechanistic and parameter-free explanation of loss-aversion. People, but not machines, thus seek the greater good, but do so under perceptual noise.

articles.title: Training data limits the prediction of consumer heterogeneity by LLM-based digital twins
articles.authors: Antonia Krefeld-Schwalb; Eric J. Johnson; Caroline Weaver; Dirk U. Wulff
articles.abstract: There is significant interest and investment in using digital twins—large language models (LLMs) supplemented by data about individuals—to replace traditional data collection in market research. Digital twins promise to offer scalable, fast, and cost-efficient alternatives to human samples. While some research suggests good aggregate accuracy, we examine whether twins inform a core goal of marketing: modeling heterogeneity in preferences and in responsiveness to marketing instruments. We evaluate the LLM's ability to represent heterogeneity in two datasets. First, we analyze the Centaur model finetuned on large-scale behavioral data in an intertemporal choice task. Second, we examine persona-prompted digital twins from the Twin-2K-500 dataset, predicting price sensitivity and 14 between-subject interventions. Centaur, trained with choices in the same task, recovered variation across humans. The persona based twins, however, showed weak correspondence to individual-level human responses and limited ability to predict how covariates (such as income) affect behavior in tasks that were not part of the training data. Overall, weight-based twins may approach human-like heterogeneity when fine-tuned on task-relevant behavioral data, and persona-based twins may retain value for coarser tasks such as aggregate demand estimation, but neither is currently an adequate substitute for humans in segmentation and targeting.

articles.title: Think Political Leader — Think He or She? A Multi-Country Test of a Gender Fair Language Intervention for Stereotype Bias
articles.authors: Kim Olivia Peters; Alma Jeftic; Timothy Ballard; Steven Zhou; Niklas K Steffens; Ilke Inceoglu; Michelle K. Ryan; Renata Bongiorno; Alice H. Eagly; Patrícia Arriaga
articles.abstract: Women are greatly underrepresented in positions of political leadership around the world. In seeking to explain this underrepresentation, some researchers have pointed to people’s tendencies to stereotype leaders as more similar to men than women as these tendencies can support the belief that women are unsuited to leadership. This project aimed to test whether a subtle linguistic intervention was able to ameliorate this gender bias in political leadership stereotypes across different languages and national contexts (country N = 42, sample N = 22,995). Specifically, this project examined whether gender fair language (e.g., the use of paired pronouns ‘he or she’) can reduce the tendency for people to stereotype political leaders as more similar to men than women. To increase the rigor with which these stereotypes were measured, this project complemented the dominant ‘cheap talk’ measure of personal stereotype content with a novel incentivised community measure of this content. As expected, participants stereotyped political leaders as more similar to men than women; this pattern was stronger on the incentivised measure of community stereotypes. Unexpectedly, there was no evidence that paired pronouns reduced this bias (there was weak exploratory evidence that paired nouns increased participants’ tendencies to stereotype political leaders as more similar to women). This project suggests that the ability of gender fair language interventions to ameliorate gender bias in the political leadership domain may be limited.

articles.title: Feasible Cognition Requires Latent State
articles.authors: Ian S. Howard
articles.abstract: Intelligent behavior across motor control, speech production, learning, and social cognition operates under uncertainty: sensory feedback is noisy and delayed, actions are imprecise, and observable outcomes often have ambiguous causes. When multiple latent states generate identical or sufficiently similar observations, regulation, learning, and evaluation based on behavior alone become underdetermined. We argue that inference over latent state is therefore a feasibility condition for cognition under partial observability, rather than merely a modeling convenience. Observable signals—movements, sensory inputs, speech outputs, and social actions—should be understood as noisy evidence about hidden causes, including motor state, articulatory configuration, task context, and intentions. This perspective suggests that separate psychological literatures share a common control-theoretic structure: regulation under noise, delay, ambiguity, and partial observability. It also helps explain why diverse domains exhibit robustness, tolerance of variability, context sensitivity, and structured credit assignment. These properties arise from regulation of inferred, task-relevant state, rather than precise control of observable behavior. The account is not proposed as a replacement for belief-state, internal-model, predictive-coding, or active-inference approaches, but as a cross-domain feasibility argument: latent-state representations support control, learning, and evaluation only when task-relevant hidden distinctions are detectable from observations and stabilizable or actionable. Cognition, from this view, depends on latent state estimates that render behavior, learning, and evaluation well-defined under uncertainty.

articles.title: Generative Similarity, Generalization, and Abstraction
articles.authors: Raja Marjieh; Sreejan Kumar; Declan Campbell; Liyi Zhang; Jake Snell; Thomas L. Griffiths
articles.abstract: Determining the degree of similarity between different stimuli is central for intelligent behavior. Psychologists have long debated the nature of similarity, considering whether it is continuous and space-like or discrete and feature-like. Here, we argue that the idea that stimuli are similar to the extent that they are likely to have been generated by the same process unifies these spatial and featural accounts as special cases. We formulate this notion of generative similarity in terms of Bayesian inference over hierarchical generative processes and show how it can illuminate key ideas from the literature including the universal law of generalization, feature contrast models, exemplar and prototype theories, and metric violations in semantic organization. Moreover, just as previous work on similarity drew on connections to multi-dimensional scaling and additive clustering, generative similarity provides a new way to construct representations of stimuli by drawing on contrastive learning, a modern machine learning procedure for learning representations by pushing similar stimuli together and dissimilar stimuli apart. We show how this approach can provide a way to translate hierarchical generative processes into spatial representations that support human-like abstraction and generalization, even in complex domains that are parameterized by probabilistic programs.

articles.title: Maternal Early Pregnancy Tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) Metabolites Correlate with Newborn Resting-State Functional Connectivity
articles.authors: Chaela Nutor; Jocelyn Stanfield; Patricia Brennan; Dana Boyd Barr; Anne Dunlop; Cassandra Hendrix
articles.abstract: Growing evidence in preclinical and clinical models suggests that prenatal cannabis exposure (PCE) is associated with changes in brain functioning and behavior. This observational longitudinal study examines these associations  using biological measures of  PCE in a sample of Black mother-infant dyads (N=42), who have historically been underrepresented in developmental neuroimaging studies. Maternal urine samples collected during pregnancy (8-14 week’ gestation) were assayed for 11-nor-9-carboxy-Δ9-tetrahydrocannabinol (COOH-THC) metabolites, providing a biologically verified measure of prenatal cannabis exposure. Newborns (57% female) completed a resting-state fMRI scan and were assessed using the NICU Network Neurobehavioral Scale at approximately 1-month postpartum. We performed voxelwise seed connectivity analyses using seeds in the bilateral hippocampus, insula, and caudate.
