We found that COOH-THC levels in early pregnancy were significantly associated with altered connectivity patterns in the hippocampus, insula, and caudate in infants. These functional connectivity patterns, however, were not correlated with indices of newborn neurobehavior. These preliminary results suggest that cannabis exposure early in pregnancy may impact neural connectivity shortly after birth, with effects that withstand influence of co-occurring maternal psychosocial factors.

articles.title: False discovery rate correction promotes confounded neuroimaging designs
articles.authors: Mark Allen Thornton
articles.abstract: False discovery rate (FDR) corrections are a popular and pragmatic approach to dealing with the problem of multiple comparisons in neuroimaging research. This paper describes a potentially problematic outcome of FDR correction. Specifically, the use of FDR will tend to promote the publication of poorly controlled experiments. This occurs because the presence of significant hypothesis tests in some parts of the brain will lower the threshold for significance elsewhere in the brain. In general, this adaptive thresholding is a feature of FDR. However, it can be problematic when a confounded design produces spurious activations, because they will lower the threshold for significance elsewhere in the brain, relative to a well-controlled study. Here I demonstrate this basic phenomenon using Monte Carlo simulations. I conduct further simulations to estimate how this phenomenon could affect more realistic confounding scenarios and how it could interact with publication bias. I conclude by suggesting mitigation approaches.

articles.title: Are Rare Events Under- or Overweighted in Decisions from Experience? The CPT Estimation Puzzle
articles.authors: Ido Erev; Eden Heilprin; Nuno Busch; Thorsten Pachur
articles.abstract: Analyses of decisions from experience based on free sampling suggest an apparent inconsistency regarding the weighting of rare events. Whereas analyses of choice behavior suggest underweighting of low probability events, analyses that model choices based on the sampled outcome distribution (experienced rare events) using cumulative prospect theory’s (CPT) probability-weighting function often suggest overweighting. We dissect this CPT estimation puzzle and argue that it reflects the joint impact of two properties of decisions based on free sampling. The first is a tendency to stop sampling rather early (implying reliance on small samples), leading to an underrepresentation of objectively rare outcomes in the information decision makers have available about the options. The second property involves the decision maker’s effort to counteract the bias implied by small samples by boosting the weight assigned to the most extreme outcomes (corrective overweighting). In computer simulations, we show that these two factors provide sufficient conditions for the emergence of the CPT estimation puzzle. In analyses of three empirical datasets, we show that—as predicted by our two-properties assertion—the puzzle does not emerge uniformly but primarily when decision makers draw medium sample sizes. We discuss the conditions under which people might apply corrective overweighting in decisions from experience.

articles.title: Basslines, Bodies, and Beehive Haircuts: What do we think about when we listen to music?
articles.authors: Hazel Aileen van der Walle; Elizabeth Hellmuth Margulis; Kelly Jakubowski
articles.abstract: Music influences distinct and specific mental content, including fictional narratives, autobiographical recall, and visual mental imagery. These imaginative responses are relevant in specialised contexts like therapy and play a vital role in everyday cognition, creativity, memory, attention, and well‑being. However, prior studies are typically constrained to one isolated thought type, use limited musical diversity, or interpret intricate imaginings through quantitative ratings—flattening the complexity of these experiences. Computational qualitative analyses can process large datasets, but fail to capture contextual richness and nuance available through human-driven qualitative analysis. What is missing is an open, manual qualitative examination of the fuller breadth of music-influenced mental content (MIMC)—an approach capable of preserving the depth and human subtleties that quantitative, predefined, isolated, and computational approaches overlook. We analysed participants’ free‑response MIMC descriptions, not restricting probing to isolated types or predefined thought-type categories. Music stimuli—120 instrumental clips from the MUSIFEAST-17 stimulus set—spanned 10 stylistically diverse genres: Ambient, Classical, Electronic, Film, Funk, Jazz, Metal, Pop, 60s-pop, 80s-pop. A total of 1,786 UK and US participants aged 18–75 years listened to 10 clips (one from each genre), reported whether they experienced imaginative responses, and provided open-text descriptions. Rigorous, mixed-method qualitative analysis was conducted on 14,099 MIMC descriptions, involving multiple coding passes, multi-coder reliability checks, thematic analysis, and co-occurrence network analysis. The resulting thematic framework functions as a typology of music-influenced “thoughtscapes”, revealing multi-faceted MIMC unfolding across narrational, referential, and experiential dimensions, offering a structured account of a fuller spectrum of listeners’ imaginative experiences.

articles.title: Teacher-student relationships and adult outcomes: Developmental cascades via childhood executive function and behavioral dysregulation
articles.authors: Sammy F. Ahmed; Andrew E Koepp; Deborah Vandell
articles.abstract: Longitudinal data were examined to test associations between teacher-student relationships and adult outcomes, as well as mechanisms underlying these associations. Results from the NICHD-SECCYD (N=1364; 52% male; 76% White; 13% Black; 6% Hispanic; 5% other; data collection took place in the U.S. beginning in 1991) revealed a complex set of findings. First, teacher-student conflict and closeness during early elementary school were associated with educational attainment, occupational prestige, arrest record, and risky behavior at age 26 (r=-.21-.17, ps<.01). Next, behavioral dysregulation during middle elementary school, but not executive function, mediated associations between teacher-student conflict and age 26 outcomes. Finally, behavioral dysregulation and end of high school functioning serially mediated associations between teacher-student conflict and adult outcomes. These findings showcase the relevance of teacher-student relationships for outcomes spanning two decades of development.

articles.title: Close to me: Psychological distance and the role of citizen science in shaping trust in science
articles.authors: Kirsten Vegt; Janneke Elberse; Laurens Hessels; Natalia Zarzeczna; Bastiaan T Rutjens
articles.abstract: Citizen science (CS) is often assumed to bring science closer to citizens and strengthen trust, yet empirical evidence remains limited. This study examines how CS-experience relates to psychological distance to science (PSYDISC) and trust. In a quasi-experimental study among Dutch adults (N = 763), individuals with and without CS-experience were compared on PSYDISC and multiple forms of trust. CS-experienced participants reported lower psychological distance, which was associated with greater trust in scientists and in the epistemic authority of science. Although CS-experience was not directly associated with generalized trust, it was indirectly related to trust via psychological distance. Trust also varied by research format: collaborative projects were trusted most, primarily among CS-experienced participants. These findings suggest that CS-experience is linked to how close science is perceived to be, which plays a role in shaping trust. At the same time, broader trust in science appears to depend on additional conditions beyond participation.

articles.title: Restrict for Thinness or for Health? Liking but Not Approach Bias Differentiates Anorexia from Orthorexia Nervosa in Responses to High-Caloric Healthy vs. High-Caloric Unhealthy Foods
articles.authors: Hannah van Alebeek; Mareike Röttger; Jana Strahler; Katharina Eichin; Isabella Forwick; Jens Blechert; Ulrich Voderholzer; Julia Reichenberger
articles.abstract:
Objective: Individuals with high levels of orthorexic eating behavior (OR) show a (pathological) fixation on healthy eating, sharing characteristics with individuals with anorexia nervosa (AN). This raised debate over whether orthorexia nervosa constitutes a distinct disorder. Distinctions may lie in preference for and approach behavior toward foods differing in calorie content and healthiness. We expected reduced liking and weaker approach biases in AN and OR compared with controls; specifically for high-caloric foods in AN and for unhealthy foods in OR.
Methods: Self-reported AN (N = 35), OR (high scores on the Düsseldorf Orthorexia Scale but no AN; N = 40), and controls (no eating disorder history, N = 101) completed liking ratings and an Approach-Avoidance Task with food stimuli varying in calorie content (high vs. low) and, for high-calorie items, healthiness (healthy vs. unhealthy). 
Results: Compared to controls, AN reported lower liking for all high-caloric foods, while OR reported lower liking only for high-caloric, unhealthy foods. Accordingly, OR also liked high-caloric healthy foods more than AN. AN showed weaker approach bias than controls (mainly driven by high-caloric unhealthy foods). OR descriptively lay between both other groups, with no significant differences in approach bias to either.
Conclusion: Explicit liking ratings distinguished AN from OR aligning with their respective motivation to reduce high-caloric versus unhealthy food intake. Reduced approach bias was specific to AN, possibly reflecting rapid engagement of approach-avoidance responses to easy-to-categorize high-caloric unhealthy foods. Contrary, health-focused processing in OR may require slower deliberations that are not captured by Approach-Avoidance Tasks.

articles.title: Threat Crowds Out Solidarity: How Politicization Erodes Discursive Support for Protest
articles.authors: Artur Pokropek; Ernest Pokropek; Roman Dolata; Hubert Plisiecki
articles.abstract: Classical theories of protest reception, best articulated in Turner (1969), posit that public support emerges from competition among simultaneously active framing mechanisms whose dominance shifts as a conflict matures. Such claims have been hard to test: surveys are too sparse, static content analysis obscures dynamics, and experimental vignettes isolate single frames from the competition that defines the theory. However, high-resolution digital discourse makes fine-grained temporal testing tractable. Using 340,729 Polish-language tweets from the 2019 teachers’ strike, we operationalize Turner’s five mechanisms as daily keyword-share indicators assessed for semantic coherence using Confirmatory Factor Analysis on Word Embeddings, and track sentiment with distributed lag and counterfactual state-space models across six phase transitions. Three patterns emerge after accounting for multiple testing: threat framing surged at onset and crowded out conciliation and bargaining; resumed negotiations depressed sentiment as bargaining collapsed while conciliation doubled — a capitulation signal; and political-coalition references were strongly associated with eroded sentiment (β₀ = −0.042) while civic-solidarity references were not.

articles.title: Predictive models of mental health based on resting-state functional connectivity show limited generalizability across late childhood and early adolescence
articles.authors: Mirjam Habegger; Benjamin Garzon; Nora Maria Raschle; Todd Hare
articles.abstract:
Late childhood and adolescence are marked by substantial reorganization of the brain’s
functional connectome and the emergence of many mental health problems. Although resting-
state functional connectivity (rsFC) has been widely used to predict mental health and related
behavioral phenotypes, it remains unclear whether such models generalize across development
or instead reflect age-specific brain-behavior mappings. Here, we tested the developmental
generalizability of whole-brain rsFC-based predictive models of mental health and cognitive
ability across three timepoints spanning ages 9-14 years in the Adolescent Brain Cognitive
Development Study. Using out-of-sample prediction and cross-timepoint model transfer, we
found that rsFC significantly predicted concurrent mental health and cognitive ability within
each age group, but models trained at one age often generalized poorly to data acquired 2-4
years earlier or later, particularly for mental health. In addition to this limited transfer, we
demonstrate developmental shifts in the associations between individual functional
connections and mental health and cognitive ability. These changes occurred alongside broader
reorganization of the functional connectome, including age-related differences in global
covariance structure and cortical network segregation. Together, our findings indicate that
brain-behavior mappings of mental health are highly dynamic across late childhood and
adolescence and may challenge the assumption that predictive models or candidate neural
biomarkers for mental health disorders derived at a single age remain valid across development.

articles.title: Afterword: Coping through crisis with coronamusic
articles.authors: Niels Chr. Hansen
articles.abstract: The COVID-19 pandemic of 2020 was accompanied by a mental health epidemic creating an extensive demand for safe, widely accessible, and highly individualized coping strategies. Engaging with music in new and innovative ways became the potentially most frequent and effective solitary and virtually-collective leisure activity for maintaining psychological wellbeing during lockdown. Emerging research findings that interest in corona-themed repertoire—coined "coronamusic"—became the strongest predictor of socio-emotional coping efficacy point towards a potentially adaptive function of thematically tailored musical innovations in human coping behaviour on historical and evolutionary timescales. This afterword starts with an exemplifying overview of the musical and societal responses to the coronavirus in Denmark. Next, based on a review of the emerging research literature on the role of music for pandemic coping, a model is proposed of key structural and contextual features of the coronamusic phenomenon. This may pave the way for including musical measures in collective and individual toolboxes for managing future societal crises.

articles.title: Growing up in an Age of Economic Inequality: A Social-Cognitive Developmental Framework
articles.authors: Eddie Brummelman; Katherine McAuliffe; Jamie Amemiya
articles.abstract: Economic inequality has reached extreme levels worldwide, and children will bear its burden. How do children perceive and explain economic inequality? And how do these perceptions and explanations, in turn, shape their development? We address these questions by proposing an integrative social-cognitive developmental framework. According to the framework, when children enter a new context, they consider a series of questions: (1) Is there inequality? (2) What causes inequality? (3) What does inequality say about me? (4) What does inequality say about us? (5) And do I address inequality? We suggest that children’s perceptions and explanations of economic inequality—above and beyond exposure to the inequality in itself—have profound consequences for mental health, social relationships, motivation, and egalitarianism (e.g., whether children rectify or perpetuate inequality). Children’s perceptions and explanations, then, might serve as levers for intervention to help children navigate the current age of inequality.

articles.title: Information-Based Sequential Monitoring in Linear Models
articles.authors: Ole Schacht; Tom Loeys; Beatrijs Moerkerke; kelly Van Lancker
articles.abstract: Researchers in psychology often use statistical inference through effect size estimation and hypothesis testing. Traditionally, the required sample size for a study is determined \textit{a priori} as a combination of statistical design choices and assumed characteristics of the population. Because these population parameters are unknown, fixed-sample designs rely on assumptions that are often difficult to justify and can lead to underpowered or inefficient studies. In this paper, we discuss as an alternative a sequential approach that monitors the total Fisher information for the effect of interest, but not the estimated effect itself. Depending on the goal of the study, a target level of total Fisher information is prespecified that corresponds to obtaining either a full confidence interval width around the effect of interest with a desired width, or to a hypothesis test that achieves the target power to detect a minimally meaningful effect. We focus specifically on the setting of linear regression models with normally distributed errors. Unlike fixed-sample designs, the sequential monitoring approach does not require prior knowledge of population parameters, but the price to pay is that the final sample size is unknown during the study. We therefore propose a nonparametric bootstrapping approach to construct prediction intervals for the final sample size of the study. These intervals are based on accumulated data, shrink as the stopping rule nears and provide researchers a transparent means to potentially modify the sampling regime or the statistical design choices of the study.

articles.title: The Group Development Questionnaire Short (GDQS) Scales: Tiny-Yet-Effective Measures of Team/Small Group Development
articles.authors: Lucas Gren; Christian Jacobsson; Niklas Rydbo; Per Lenberg
articles.abstract: The Group Development Questionnaire Short (GDQS) Scales, a 13-item short form of the 60-item Group Development Questionnaire, was developed and validated across four studies. The GDQS scales with four items for Scale I, and three items for Scales II–IV, had consistent and acceptable internal consistencies across Studies 1 and 2 (α at or well above .65), similar to the parent measure. Moreover, the GDQS scales showed a comparable pattern of convergent, and criterion-related validity (Studies 1–3), and concurrent validity (Study 4). Collectively, these results indicate that the GDQS is a psychometrically acceptable and practically useful short measure of team/group development. The GDQS can freely be used for research, with written permission, and we conclude the paper with norm data and practical guidelines for researchers wanting to apply the measurement.

articles.title: Tales of Life: Curiosity for Positive and Negative Life Stories of Culturally Close and Distant Others
articles.authors: Esther Niehoff; Marret Noordewier; Malgorzata A Goclowska; Suzanne Oosterwijk
articles.abstract: Personalized stories about others’ life experiences are omnipresent in media, yet we know little about what drives people to engage with such narratives. Across two studies (N = 596 UK adults), we investigated how valence (positive versus negative life event) and cultural distance (experience from someone from a culturally close versus distant country) shape social curiosity. Consistently, participants reported greater curiosity for stories about negative life events, especially when the story’s protagonist was culturally close. Structural equation modelling revealed that the relationship between negative valence and higher curiosity was mediated through greater anticipated insight and – counterintuitive at first sight – greater anticipated distress towards negative stories. At the same time, curiosity for negative stories was attenuated through lower expected enjoyment. These findings suggest that curiosity is driven by anticipated emotional impact of either valence, and have implications for fostering empathy and meaningful engagement in media and public discourse.

articles.title: Language as both a proxy and a trigger of modern behavior in prehistory
articles.authors: Antonio Benítez-Burraco
articles.abstract: Modern behavior evolved progressively during Upper Paleolithic, with a recent outburst of complex cultural practices (including sophisticated symbolic behavior, advanced technologies, and extensive social networking) in several places, particularly in Europe. Diverse factors have been claimed to account for this trend, like climate changes, population increases, changes in human ecology, or cognitive innovations. In this paper, we argue that language could have been a facilitator, and even a trigger of such changes. Traditionally, complex, modern-like languages have been regarded just a byproduct of our cognitive and behavioral modernity. Nonetheless, culturally-complex, socially-diverse, and technologically-advanced groups benefit from rich vocabularies (that make reference more precise), potentiated categorization abilities (that create richer taxonomies), and sophisticated syntax (that allows to coin new words and convey complex information and know-hows to out-group people in more precise ways), all of which are hallmarks of (some types) of modern languages. In summary, we wish to argue that complex languages were not just an outcome of human modernity, but a key triggering factor of it.

articles.title: A Practical Unified Framework for Evaluating Psychological Theories and Observational Research Practices
articles.authors: Peter Hilpert; Matthew J Vowels
articles.abstract:
In psychology, knowledge advances primarily through theory development and empirical testing within a dominant framework: theory, hypothesis, design, measurement, modeling, and interpretation. Although rigorous execution is assumed to yield valid insights, meta-scientific critiques reveal profound shortcomings. This article addresses these by (1) identifying seven core deficiencies in theory development; (2) delineating seven critical flaws in observational empirical testing; and (3) introducing three coherence dimensions (conceptual, causal, technical) across the pipeline as an integrative requirement. We synthesize these previously fragmented critiques into a novel, unified evaluative framework consisting of 14 explicit evaluation criteria plus three coherence dimensions. For each component we derive concrete, manuscript-level assessment criteria that can be applied at the sentence and section level. We demonstrate the framework’s practical utility by applying it to two published empirical papers, revealing detectable issues even in high-quality work. 
Compounded with well-documented problems such as low statistical power (~36%) and replication rates of 30–50%, these upstream deficiencies likely render a substantial proportion of published findings fragile or uninterpretable. Importantly, these flaws lie upstream of replication: while replication is essential for assessing reproducibility and can motivate theory revision, it cannot remedy deeper theoretical and methodological weaknesses and may, in some cases, reinforce flawed conclusions. By providing a practical tool for structured assessment and reform, this framework aims to strengthen theory construction, empirical rigor, and cumulative progress in psychology and related social sciences.

articles.title: How well do linguistic systems fulfil their functions? A typology of linguistic quality measures
articles.authors: Charles Kemp; John Mansfield
articles.abstract: Language supports communication, thought, and social interaction, and characterizing these functions can help to explain the properties of linguistic systems. We provide a typology of approaches that aim to evaluate how well linguistic systems fulfil their functions, and discuss how these approaches have been used to study cross-linguistic variation and language change. We focus on quantitative approaches that include formal measures of the quality or goodness of a system, and include several case studies related to phonology, syntax, and semantics. The approaches we consider are currently scattered across the literature in a way that impedes progress, and we discuss the prospects for developing more integrated theories of linguistic quality.

articles.title: Six Dimensions or Four? A Psychometric Re-examination of Hofstede's Values Survey Module 2013 in a Contemporary U.S. Sample
articles.authors: Nathan Iverson
articles.abstract: Hofstede's Values Survey Module 2013 (VSM 2013) is the most widely used instrument for measuring national cultural values, yet recent psychometric work has raised concerns about whether its six dimensions are empirically separable. The present study examines VSM 2013's measurement properties in a contemporary U.S. Prolific sample (N = 185). Three converging lines of evidence indicate that the published six-factor structure does not fit. First, internal consistency is poor across all six dimensions (Cronbach's alphas from -.37 to .53), and reverse-coding items per the formula's implied keying makes reliability worse for five of six dimensions, with two going negative. Second, parallel analysis recommends four factors, and the resulting exploratory solution does not recover Hofstede's groupings; items load on a different and mostly cleaner structure mixing dimensions. Third, confirmatory factor analysis of the published six-factor model produces inadmissible parameter estimates (factor correlations of 1.42 and 1.15, both Heywood cases) alongside poor fit (CFI = .60, RMSEA = .075), while a data-suggested four-factor structure fits substantially better (CFI = .84, RMSEA = .07) with all parameters in bounds. We discuss implications for cross-cultural research using VSM 2013 country scores and outline a confirmatory replication agenda.

articles.title: Explanation Generation: Questions Direct Exploration in Semantic Space
articles.authors: Kara Kedrick; Russell Golman
articles.abstract: In everyday reasoning and scientific inquiry, explanations help people make sense of their experiences and observations. We investigate how people generate new possible explanations. Specifically, we study how the phrasing of “why” questions shapes exploration of the space of possible explanations.  We hypothesized that semantic exploration is guided by words that appear earlier, are more surprising, and are more familiar. Participants in our experiment generated explanations in response to “why” questions comparing two words, with the order of these two comparison words randomized. Using a large language model, we measured how closely each explanation aligned with the comparison words. Results supported two of three hypotheses: explanations aligned more strongly with earlier and more familiar words. These findings highlight how subtle phrasing of a question can shape explanation generation, demonstrating context-dependence in how people explore a conceptual space.

articles.title: What AI publishing policies get wrong
articles.authors: Zhicheng Lin
articles.abstract: AI policies in scholarly publishing often regulate large-language-model (LLM) use through claims about what such systems can understand or know and what their deployments retain or expose. Yet many of these claims are technically inaccurate or unstable (i.e., contingent on model version, interface, retrieval access, provider terms, and data-handling configuration). Analyzing policies from 13 publishers and societies—including SAGE, the Association for Psychological Science, the American Psychological Association, PNAS, Science, Nature/Springer Nature, Elsevier/Lancet, and Wiley—I identify recurring mismatches between capability assumptions and prescribed rules across six domains: capability framing, use categorization, prohibited uses, confidentiality, disclosure, and attribution. I propose a mechanism- and risk-based framework organized around two dimensions: material contribution to the scientific record and confidentiality risk. The framework yields four editorial stances—routine copyediting, disclosed substantive AI assistance, approved confidential deployments, and prohibition when sensitive materials would enter unsafe systems—and, applied to existing policies, produces technology-agnostic guidance that preserves human accountability, protects confidential materials, and remains robust as AI systems and workflows change.

articles.title: Latent Profiles of Personality and Imaginative Thinking: Student Differences in Enrollment Across College Disciplines
articles.authors: Kristen Lamb; Lindsay Ellis Lee
articles.abstract: In a society where creativity is increasingly emphasized, imagination remains underexamined despite its foundational role in creative processes. This study used a person-centered approach to examine latent personality and imaginative capability profiles, sex differences, and how these profiles relate to students’ enrollment across college disciplines. Latent Profile Analysis identified three typologies among university students: Profile 1, Reactive Realists (10.5%), characterized by lower imagination and emotional stability; Profile 2, Reserved Idealists (53.3%), characterized by lower extraversion and moderately elevated imagination; and Profile 3, Balanced Visionaries (36.2%), characterized by high imagination and greater emotional stability. Profiles were primarily differentiated by overall levels rather than distinct patterns across traits. Although chi-square analyses indicated statistically significant differences in profile membership by sex, these effects were small. Multinomial regression analyses showed that profile membership, sex, and race/ethnicity were associated with discipline enrollment. Balanced Visionaries had lower odds of enrollment in the College of Science relative to Liberal Arts and Social Sciences. Females were less likely to enroll in Business, Engineering, and Music, but more likely to enroll in Education and Visual Arts and Design. These findings highlight the value of person-centered approaches while also showing that sex and race more consistently predict college discipline enrollment.

articles.title: Neutral Cue Autobiographical Narratives as a Discourse Assessment Tool for Detecting Latent Aphasia
articles.authors: Deborah Levy; Katelyn Urena; Kent Meinert; Davida Fromm; Brian MacWhinney; Brielle C Stark
articles.abstract:
Purpose: “Latent aphasia” describes an acquired language impairment (e.g., after stroke) that is not detectable on standardized language evaluations. Discourse tasks such as the Cinderella story are commonly used and are effective at revealing latent aphasia. However, that task, in particular, is limited by its childlike nature and assumption of shared cultural knowledge. Alternatively, a Neutral Cue task—drawn from the neuropsychology literature—provides participants with neutral cue words and prompts them to respond with a relevant autobiographical narrative. Like Cinderella, this task elicits abundant memory-based connected speech, yet does not suffer from the same set of limitations; it also engages first person positioning and establishing of common ground.
Method: The Cinderella story task and the Neutral Cue task were administered to 72 individuals (26 with latent aphasia, 46 with no aphasia) on two occasions separated by 5-10 days. Sessions were videotaped and later transcribed for microlinguistic and psycholinguistic analyses to distinguish between groups and establish test-retest reliability.
Results: Five metrics—mean length of utterance, number of tokens produced, words per minute, verbs per utterance, and percent word error—reliably distinguished between latent aphasia and language in individuals with no neurological impairments across both tasks. First person positioning and markers for establishing common ground were more pronounced in the Neutral Cue than the Cinderella task.
Conclusions: These results suggest that the Neutral Cue task may be a viable complement to the Cinderella story for detecting latent aphasia, with particular strengths in investigating discourse skills critical to everyday life.

articles.title: The CMEBE model: an integrative embodied account of behaviour and behaviour change
articles.authors: Saskia Sanderson
articles.abstract: Behavioural models and theories are useful because they help researchers, practitioners and policy-makers understand human behaviour and characterise behaviour change interventions. Such frameworks guide decisions about where to focus efforts and finite resources – across individual, community and population levels. However, existing models of behaviour often prioritise social and psychological constructs over the embodied aspects of individuals. This paper introduces a new approach: the CMEBE model – an integrative, embodied account of human behaviour grounded in behavioural science and informed by neuroscience. In the CMEBE model, behaviour emerges as a dynamic process arising from continuous interactions among four systems which together bridge experience, embodiment and context: the Conscious Mind (CM), Embodied Brain (E1), and Body (B) which are located within the individual, and the Environment (E2) which is external to the individual. By explicitly situating consciousness within the broader biological and environmental systems from which it arises, the CMEBE model bridges methodological and explanatory gaps between lived experiences and mechanistic accounts of behaviour and behaviour change. The CMEBE model offers an integrative, embodied approach to behaviour and behaviour change for researchers, practitioners and policy-makers. Ultimately, the test of the CMEBE model will be whether it explains behaviour more effectively and supports clearer design, targeting and characterisation of behaviour change interventions than existing models.

articles.title: The role of subcortical brain tissue iron as an indicator of dopamine neurophysiology in adolescent cannabis use
articles.authors: Sarah Ann Thomas; Meghan A. Gonsalves; Gillian LeBlanc; Elizabeth Lorenc; Jane Metrik; Michael Frank; Sarah Ryan; Emily Olenik; Leslie Brick; Anthony Spirito
articles.abstract: Approximately 10-20% of U.S. adolescents report past-year cannabis use (CU). Although regular CU beginning in adolescence is expected to blunt dopamine-related neurophysiology, this hypothesis has not been tested in adolescents due to methodological limitations. However, neurophysiology contributing to dopamine can be noninvasively indexed via subcortical tissue iron measured with magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). We examined adolescent CU quantity, frequency, and problems in relation to tissue iron in regions with high dopamine activity, hypothesizing that greater CU would be linked to less tissue iron. Adolescents (n=81; 64.2% female) aged 14-17 reporting either fewer than 5 lifetime cannabis episodes (n=47) or more than 11 episodes (n=34), with limited alcohol and nicotine use and no other illicit substance use, completed substance use assessments and an MRI. We calculated the inverse of the normalized T2* measurement (1/nT2*; lower values indicate less tissue iron) from resting-state functional scans by assessing relative T2* decay. 1/nT2* was estimated using subcortical masks for hypothesized regions. Lower 1/nT2* signal was associated with increased daily concentrate hits (b = -0.01, p  < 0.001), cannabis hours high (b = -0.01, p = 0.016), CU frequency (b = -0.01, p = 0.01), and cannabis use disorder (CUD) severity (b = -0.01, p = 0.003). Post-hoc analyses highlighted the VTA as a key region. Results align with reduced dopamine-related neurophysiology associated with CU in adult and animal samples, and have implications for understanding adolescent CUD development. Measuring 1/nT2* offers an innovative, non-invasive method to index neurobiological alterations in adolescent CU.

articles.title: What You See is What You Regret
articles.authors: Kate Petrova; Nick Madibekov; Tobias Gerstenberg; James Gross
articles.abstract: Regret is a counterfactual emotion that arises when one judges that an outcome would have been better if one had acted differently. Classic accounts distinguish between commission regret (regretting an action) and omission regret (regretting an inaction). Here, we re-examine this distinction through the lens of attention and counterfactual salience. We find that externally highlighting subsets of options reliably increased omission and commission regret even after action. Increasing the size of the salient set attenuated regret intensity. When participants actively constructed their own consideration sets, the same patterns emerged. These findings suggest that commission--omission asymmetries may depend less on action versus inaction per se, and more on how attention shapes the space of salient counterfactuals, with implications for when regret might support adaptive learning or disrupt it.

articles.title: Beyond Behavioral Signs: Toward a Multidomain Risk Architecture for Early Detection of Autism Spectrum
articles.authors: Pedro Salinas Quintana
articles.abstract:
Background: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) prevalence has increased markedly over recent decades, yet primary tools for early detection remain focused almost exclusively on observable behavioral signs in children aged 18 months and older. Instruments such as the M-CHAT-R/F, ADOS-2, and ADI-R, while clinically valuable, operate within a narrow temporal and conceptual window that systematically excludes a wide range of prenatal, perinatal, medical, environmental, and psychosocial risk factors now supported by robust empirical evidence.
Objective: This paper presents a theoretically grounded critique of current early detection frameworks for ASD and proposes a Multidomain Early Risk Architecture (MERA)—a conceptual model integrating ten empirically supported domains of risk that precede or co-occur with the emergence of behavioral signs.
Methods: A comprehensive narrative review of peer-reviewed literature published primarily between 2006 and 2025 was conducted across PubMed, PsycINFO, and Web of Science. Sources were organized into ten thematic domains: (1) genetic and family history, (2) prenatal and perinatal conditions, (3) prenatal substance and toxic exposures, (4) early medical conditions, (5) psychomotor and language development, (6) early social behavior, (7) restricted and repetitive behaviors, (8) atypical sensory processing, (9) feeding and gastrointestinal functioning, and (10) psychosocial and environmental factors.
Results: Evidence from meta-analyses, large population-based cohorts, and longitudinal studies consistently demonstrates that ASD risk is distributed across multiple domains detectable before the classic behavioral phenotype consolidates. Across 176 peer-reviewed references, pre-behavioral domains (D1–D4) account for 38% of the total evidence base, representing risk factors systematically missed by current instruments.
Conclusion: The MERA model has direct implications for screening instrument development, primary care protocols, and public health policy—particularly in settings such as Chile where diagnostic delays of two to four years remain common. Future research should validate the MERA framework prospectively and develop tiered risk-stratification instruments based on its domain structure.

articles.title: Hunger as an uncontroversial predictor of poor adolescent mental health: evidence from a multiverse analysis of 410,213 adolescents across 79 countries
articles.authors: Mirela Zaneva; Tsvetomira Dumbalska; Aaron Reeves; Lucy Bowes
articles.abstract: Background: Hunger has established detrimental impacts on physical health, with emerging evidence indicating negative impacts on mental health. However, there is a pronounced knowledge gap outside high-income settings and for adolescents. Previous research also provides differing estimates of hunger’s impacts, potentially underpinned by a wide range of researcher degrees of freedom. Methods: We investigated the relationship between hunger and mental health (proxied by single-item indicator for worrying, and three items for suicidality) in the Global School-based Student Health Survey, combining datasets from 79 countries and in a pooled sample of 410,213 adolescents (mean age 14.72 ±1.56 years; 52% girls). We used a principled multiverse analysis, exploring 15,360 model specifications. Results: Our results show a consistently stable negative relationship between hunger and adolescent mental health (worrying, median beta = .12; suicidality, median beta = .07). This reflects 12 and 7 percentage point increases in the prevalence for worry and suicidality, respectively, when experiencing hunger. This relationship is significant across a variety of analytical choices including sample selection, covariate choice (including country-level controls for wealth and inequality), predictor and outcome manipulation, among others. In a further multiverse analysis, we find evidence of a dose-response relationship, such that more frequently experiencing hunger is associated with greater reported levels of worrying and suicidality. Conclusions: Our work attests to the experience of hunger as one of the most clearly uncontroversial predictors of mental health. Addressing food insecurity and the equitable and efficient distribution of food globally, is a crucial consideration for adolescent mental health protection and suicide prevention. Emphasis should be placed on appropriate population-level interventions, such as universal free school or community meal programs.

articles.title: Social and Community Influences on Language Switching  in Bilingual Toddlers’ Everyday Input
articles.authors: Jessica Elizabeth Kosie; Laia Fibla; Rachel Ka Ying Tsui; Christine Potter; Krista Byers-Heinlein; Casey Lew-Williams
articles.abstract: Language switching is a central feature of bilingual toddlers’ input, yet the everyday patterns that infants experience remain poorly understood. To characterize the switching that bilingual toddlers’ encounter, we recorded naturalistic play interactions on two occasions: a dyadic session between the toddler and a primary caregiver and a multi-person session that included additional household members. We calculated the number of language switches per minute produced by speakers in toddlers’ environments and classified these switches as within-speaker (a single person alternating languages) or across-speaker (different speakers using different languages in sequence). Patterns of language switching were then compared across Spanish-English (New Jersey) and French-English (Montreal) bilingual communities. Compared to sessions with only a primary caregiver, toddlers heard approximately 50% more speech and twice as much language switching in sessions with multiple household members present. This shift in switching was primarily driven by increased across-speaker switches, a pattern that was especially pronounced in the French-English community. Although there was considerable variability in switching across families, primary caregivers showed relatively stable switching behavior across sessions, suggesting that they may provide a source of consistency within otherwise dynamic language environments. Together, these findings underscore the importance of recognizing that language learning unfolds within interpersonal contexts, and that variation in social configurations may be particularly consequential for bilingual learners’ language input.

articles.title: Causal language about social interactions
articles.authors: Verona Teo; Claire Augusta Bergey; Tobias Gerstenberg
articles.abstract: Causal language is central to our understanding of social interactions—whether someone “caused” or “allowed” another’s action shifts our impression of what happened. Yet models of causal language use have largely focused on physical events (e.g., billiard balls), ignoring the beliefs and preferences implicated in human action. We present a computational framework and three experiments investigating how people use causal expressions (“caused,” “enabled,” “allowed,” “made no difference”) across physical, epistemic, and preference-based interventions between agents. We find that people prefer different causal expressions across these intervention types: they describe removing a physical obstacle as a different form of facilitation than providing information. We capture people’s language use with a model that selects utterances based on counterfactual simulations of events, inferences about agents’ mental states, and utterance informativity. This model explains human judgments better than baseline models, suggesting that describing social influence involves reasoning about mental states, alternative actions, and alternative utterances.

articles.title: Altered speech rhythm in children with developmental language disorder improved by rhythmic priming
articles.authors: Julie Camici; Anna Fiveash; Oussama Abdoun; Anahita Basirat; Nathalie Bedoin; Barbara Tillmann; Anne Kösem
articles.abstract: Developmental Language Disorder (DLD) is characterized by impairments in speech comprehension and/or production that cannot be explained by hearing loss, intellectual deficits or neurological damage. While difficulties in phonology, semantic, and syntactic abilities have been extensively studied, prosodic aspects, in particular speech rhythm production, remain underexplored. For this aim, we analyzed spoken sentence productions of French-speaking children with and without DLD. Participants repeated sentences after having listened to either regular or irregular rhythmic primes. We examined (1) speech rhythm production, (2) the influence of rhythmic priming on speech production measures, and (3) the influence of covariates related to cognitive development on speech rhythm production and the rhythmic priming effect. Principal Component Analysis (PCA) was performed on twenty-four speech rhythm metrics, calculated from the duration of distinct linguistic units (i.e., vowels, consonants, syllables, words, pauses). PCA results showed that the dimensionality of speech rhythmic measures can be reduced to four main dimensions, regrouping speech rate, syllable variability, vocalic proportion and variability, and duration of pauses, respectively. The results showed that (1) children with DLD had slower speech rate and higher syllable variability compared to the control group, (2) exposure to regular rhythmic primes increased speech rate in children with DLD, whose rhythmic patterns became more similar to those of the control group, and (3) interindividual differences modulated speech rhythm patterns and effects of rhythmic priming. Our findings encourage the use of speech rhythm measures in the characterization of DLD, and the use of rhythmic stimulation in training and rehabilitation.

articles.title: How do brain maps affect neuroscientific investigation?
articles.authors: Marina Dubova; Robert Goldstone
articles.abstract: This study explores how scientific simplifications, such as partitioning the brain into distinct regions, shape scientific investigation. One hundred fifty-six psychology undergraduates (novices) and thirty-one neuroscientists (experts) explored a fictional brain segment using neuroimaging and lesioning experiments. Experts engaged in broader exploration than novices, with experimental diversity positively correlating with learning outcomes for both groups. The brain’s partitioning into regions affected participants’ experimental choices and learning success, though these effects varied across individuals. Novices favored testing boundaries between regions, while both groups focused on prototypical “skeletal" locations. These biases affected learning success: novices excelled at identifying signals near boundaries, whereas experts were better at locating signals near the centers of regions. Additionally, both groups demonstrated conceptual expectations associating a discovered signal with only one region and assuming a one-to-one mapping between signals and behavioral tasks. Understanding how simplifications influence scientific experimentation is key to re-engineering the conceptual foundations of science.

articles.title: The Ghost in the Consulting Room: A Critical Review of Clinical Neutrality as a Technology of Depoliticisation
articles.authors: Rashi Amberray Ulman
articles.abstract: This paper interrogates the ideological and clinical limits of therapeutic neutrality within the South Asian psychotherapeutic context, arguing that neutrality often functions not as an ethical absence of bias but as a technology of depoliticisation that reproduces caste and gender hierarchies. Specifically, the paper examines how “neutral” therapeutic frameworks within Indian clinical psychology frequently individualise structurally produced suffering, thereby obscuring the psychopolitical realities of Brahminical patriarchy, caste-based humiliation, and gendered violence. Using a critical theoretical review methodology, the paper synthesises scholarship from Dalit-Feminist standpoint theory, decolonial psychology, liberation psychology, psychoanalysis, and community mental health. It traces the historical emergence of neutrality and examines how diagnostic language, professional detachment, and the “blank screen” ideal transform social suffering into individual pathology. The review identifies therapeutic neutrality as a structurally mediated stance that can reinforce epistemic violence by silencing the political realities shaping distress. In response, the paper proposes an emancipatory framework grounded in collective witnessing, Ambedkarite dialogical praxis, and the concept of the “Social Third.” Ultimately, the paper contributes to decolonial critiques of mental health by advocating for a contextually situated, caste-conscious, and ethically reflexive model of clinical psychology within the Global South.

articles.title: Shared and Unique Contributions of Interoceptive Confusion and Alexithymia to Sensory Processing Differences in Autism
articles.authors: Stefen Beeler-Duden; Sophie Lawson; Ishita Bakshi; Mirella Dapretto; Shulamite Green; Abha Gupta; Lauren Kenworthy; Goldie McQuaid; Jennifer MacCormack; Kevin Pelphrey
articles.abstract: Sensory processing differences are a core feature of autism, yet mechanisms underlying sensory distress remain incompletely understood. Emerging work suggests that interoceptive difficulties and alexithymia, reflecting uncertainty about bodily and emotional states, may be associated with sensory responsivity. The present study examined the independent and shared associations of interoceptive confusion and alexithymia with sensory processing differences in 69 autistic adolescents and young adults (M age = 20.92 years). Participants completed self-report measures of sensory responsivity (Glasgow Sensory Questionnaire; GSQ), interoceptive confusion (ISQ-8), and alexithymia (GAFS-8). In multiple regression analyses, both alexithymia (B = .42, p < .001) and interoceptive confusion (B = .36, p = .001) were independently associated with greater sensory processing differences, together accounting for approximately half of the variance (R² ≈ .49). Commonality analyses indicated that most explained variance was shared (~58%), alongside unique contributions from alexithymia (~24%) and interoceptive confusion (~18%). Associations were largely domain-general across sensory modalities and hyper- and hypo-responsivity dimensions. To our knowledge, this is the first study to partition shared and unique contributions of interoceptive confusion and alexithymia to sensory responsivity in autism. These findings support integrative accounts linking sensory processing differences to internal state uncertainty and highlight interoceptive and emotional awareness as dimensions relevant to sensory experience in autism.

articles.title: Responsibility for influencing others
articles.authors: Chuqi Hu; Tobias Gerstenberg; Sarah A Wu
articles.abstract: Collective outcomes often result from complex social dynamics where individuals both contribute directly and also shape each other's contributions. How do we hold people responsible for an outcome when their actions influence others? Here, we examine how an individual's role within a group (whether they can influence others, be influenced by others, or act independently) affects how responsible they are judged to be. Across three experiments spanning both social and physical settings, we find that people systematically assign greater responsibility to those who can influence others. Furthermore, influencers with knowledge of their potential impact were held more responsible than those who were unaware. The relative responsibility of individuals who were influenced by others and who acted independently differed by context. Together, these results show that we hold others responsible by considering not only how their actions directly affect the outcome, but also how they affect others' propensity to act.

articles.title: The pitfalls of pay-to-play morality
articles.authors: Ryan W. Carlson; Molly Crockett
articles.abstract: Morality is often measured in dollars and cents. Economic games and charitable giving tasks frame giving money to strangers as moral and keeping money as selfish. This ‘pay-to-play’ paradigm has grown popular with the rise of online experiments, yet its underlying assumptions often go untested. Here, we present data that complicate the interpretation of this paradigm. We invited US crowdworkers (N=1384) to make charitable decisions and measured not just their choices, but the motives and meanings they ascribed to those choices. We found that crowdworkers’ most common motive for keeping money was acute financial need (e.g., struggling to pay for medical care or groceries). These individuals reported significantly lower incomes, and did not view their decisions to keep money as selfish. Third-party judges (N=140) agreed that keeping money in economic tasks out of financial need was not selfish, and considered such tasks to be an unreasonable measure of altruism and selfishness. Finally, we found that crowdworkers reporting financial need were unresponsive to standard interventions designed to motivate generosity. Overall, these findings challenge the assumption that keeping money uniformly reflects selfishness, and raise ethical and practical questions about how experimenters should define, measure, and motivate moral behavior.

articles.title: Segmentation and control: Control over transitions differentially impacts within-event memory
articles.authors: Ashwin Srinivasan; Jeffrey M. Zacks
articles.abstract:
People segment everyday life into meaningful units, called events. This process is largely
implicit and often happens when there are marked changes in the environment. Prior work has
shown that that people remember boundaries between events better than event middles; however,
in most previous studies the participant has been a passive observer of events, and changes in the
environment have been determined solely by the experimenter. In everyday experience, people
often have some control over the timing of salient changes—for example, walking from one
room to another. In this experiment, participants watched sequences of videos; one group of
participants could actively choose when to transition to the next video, while another
experienced the same transitions without being able to control their timing. A third group also
watched passively but was given a countdown indicating when the current video would end. All
three groups then performed recall and recognition tests. Importantly, targets in the recognition
task came from the beginning, middle, and end of each participant’s viewing of the videos. The
active group performed significantly better than the passive and countdown groups, and
beginnings were remembered better than endings. Active participants remembered ends of events
better than passive or countdown participants but remembered event beginnings worse, in both
recognition and recall. These data suggest that control of environmental changes shapes the
structure of episodic memory, whereas the predictability of when changes will occur does not.

articles.title: Person-Specific Personality Network Dynamics as Predictors of Distal Outcomes
articles.authors: Adam Taylor Nissen; Simran Johal; Emorie D Beck
articles.abstract: A primary goal of personality psychology is understanding the predictive power of personality in people’s lives. Much work has linked personality traits to life outcomes, but little has linked person-specific personality dynamics to life outcomes to provide a more nuanced approach that tracks with daily behavior. Yet, previous studies linking personality dynamics to outcomes only examined these associations cross-sectionally, making it unclear whether person-specific dynamics also predict more distal outcomes. In this exploratory study, we investigated prospective associations between individual differences in person-specific dynamics, conceptualized as personality network parameters, with 13 life outcomes over two years. Our findings indicated that node and edge properties in the network were the most robust predictors of distal outcomes, hinting that the accumulation of specific node dynamics over time may link personality traits to outcomes. We discuss the implications of these findings for personality theory and for policy and intervention.

articles.title: Longitudinal Changes in Self-Control and Midfrontal Theta During Incarceration and Release.
articles.authors: Victoria RAMBAUD; Ilke Veeckman; Louis Favril; Tom Vander Beken; Emilie A. Caspar
articles.abstract:
Self-control (SC), the ability to resist impulses and initiate goal-directed behaviour, is central to criminal behaviour and rehabilitation. However, it remains unclear whether incarceration impacts SC. 
In this pre-registered longitudinal study, newly-detained (incoming, n = 67), and newly-released individuals (outgoing, n = 25), along with community controls (n = 40) completed a cognitive task measuring SC with electroencephalography. Frontal theta activity was recorded as a marker of cognitive control. Participants were reassessed after four months, and incarcerated groups after eight additional months. Perceptions of prison environment and post-release quality of life were collected using validated questionnaires.
Behaviourally, SC remained stable in the incoming group, whereas controls declined over time. The outgoing group showed a selective decline in initiatory SC following release. Across groups, inhibitory SC was lower than initiatory SC, indicating greater difficulty resisting impulses. At the neural level, the incoming group exhibited reduced theta activity, reflecting automatization, while theta remained stable in the other groups. SC was primarily associated with individual characteristics, whereas environmental factors showed delayed effects. 
While incarceration appears to preserve or automatize SC, release selectively challenges initiatory SC. Behaviourally and neurally, SC appears primarily shaped by individual factors, with environmental influences emerging more gradually over time.

articles.title: Semantic Cognition for and from Language Models
articles.authors: Kanishka Misra
articles.abstract: The success of neural network language models (LMs) at showing semantically sophisticated behavior raises questions about the extent to which they are compatible with human conceptual knowledge, and if they can perhaps also advance our understanding of semantic cognition. This brief opinion explores this potentially bidirectional connection between LMs and semantic cognition. Specifically, I review a range of methodology used to establish the structure and the function of conceptual knowledge in LMs, primarily inspired by the semantic cognition literature. I then summarize three possible ways that future work could take to make contributions in the opposite direction, by using insights from language models to make conclusions about the language-based origin and mechanisms of human semantic cognition.

articles.title: A comprehensive systematic review of the psychometrics of left-wing ideology
articles.authors: Diego Avendaño; Ezequiel Lopez-Lopez; Angel Sánchez-Zavala; Piero Gayozzo; Laureano Brisuela-Blume; Juan Díaz; J. Francisco Santibáñez-Palma; Amauri Martínez-Vázquez; Víctor García-Belaunde; Edwar Arango
articles.abstract: This PRISMA-compliant systematic review synthesized the psychometric landscape of instruments used to measure left-wing ideologies. We analyzed 1,899 articles published between 1930 and 2023, comprising 2,024 original samples and over 2.4 million participants. Eight constructs were examined: political orientation, operational ideology, feminism, environmentalism, egalitarianism, communism, left-wing authoritarianism, and progressivism. Results showed a heavy reliance on single-item measures, substantial conceptual and methodological fragmentation, and generally limited psychometric validation. Computational linguistic analyses indicated that, despite more developed multi-item models in feminism and environmentalism, measurement is increasingly based on self-identification rather than ideologically grounded content. Operational ideology, communism, and egalitarianism lack established measurement models, while left-wing authoritarianism and progressivism are beginning to receive attention. The literature is also strongly biased toward WEIRD and university students samples, with minimal non-Western representation. Overall, the findings underscore the need for multidimensional, theory-driven, and culturally sensitive instruments to capture the complexity of left-wing ideological conceptions and guide future measurement development.

articles.title: Beyond Open Repositories: The Need for an Empirically Guided Metascientific Framework in Precision Functional Mapping
articles.authors: Daniel Kristanto
articles.abstract: Precision functional mapping (PFM) is actively shifting neuroimaging away from traditional group-average atlases to capture the unique network topology of individual brains. However, translating these personalized methods to clinical and developmental populations presents significant challenges. Recent calls for open algorithmic repositories highlight the need to manage this heterogeneity, but a static database is insufficient. This perspective paper argues that the field requires a dynamic, empirically guided metascientific framework to actively steer method development. By establishing a centralized benchmarking registry integrated with existing open-science infrastructures (e.g., OpenNeuro, EBRAINS) and standardized formats (Brain Imaging Data Structure; BIDS), the community could solve core methodological challenges in PFM. Specifically, this paper outlines how targeted evaluation criteria – such as quantifying a stability gap for data sparsity and explicitly preserving neural endophenotypes for the correspondence problem – could guide the optimization of PFM pipelines. Finally, to build a complete precision pipeline, this metascientific evaluation should expand to integrate personalized brain parcellations with heterogeneous association models and idiographic behavioral phenotyping. Establishing this community-driven ecosystem will accelerate the translation of precision neuroimaging for both clinical diagnosis and the study of human cognition.

articles.title: Changing minds without changing hearts? Experimental evidence that inoculation improves discernment of antisemitic misinformation on social media but may be limited in changing identity-driven attitudes
articles.authors: Neil Ilan Lavie-Driver; Sander van der Linden
articles.abstract: Inoculation theory has emerged as a promising pre-emptive intervention against misinformation, yet few studies have extended it to misinformation driven by social identity processes such as outgroup hostility. Here, we compare the effectiveness of inoculation against identity-salient online misinformation versus misinformation of lower identity ­salience. Study 1 (N = 250) explores antisemitic misinformation, while Study 2 (N = 250) focuses on Autism treatment misinformation. In line with research suggesting individuals may prioritise identity concerns over accuracy goals when forming their attitudes, we find that, while inoculation improves misinformation discernment about Jews online (d = 0.33, p = .01), it does not significantly reduce antisemitic attitudes (d = 0.16, p =.212). On the other hand, a similar inoculation design against Autism treatment misinformation both improves misinformation discernment about Autism treatment-related tweets (d = 0.68, p < .001) and the ability to discern between credible and uncredible treatments in a separate attitude scale (d = 0.55. p < .001). Our results suggest that while inoculation is effective in improving misinformation discernment, on its own it may be insufficient for the shifting of identity-driven attitudes. We discuss theoretical and practical implications of these findings.

articles.title: The Tip of the Iceberg? Insights into the Prevalence of Publication Bias in Two Probability-Based Academic Panels
articles.authors: Caroline Poppa; Désirée Nießen; Jessica Daikeler; Henning Silber; Bernd Weiß; David Richter
articles.abstract: Publication bias is the prioritized and selective reporting of scientifically significant results and often stems from the assumption that statistically significant research findings are rated as more important, credible, valuable, and publishable than nonsignificant or null findings. Previous work shows that this assumption makes researchers less likely to publish nonsignificant or null findings, a phenomenon termed file drawer bias. While prior research investigated the file drawer bias primarily at the study level (e.g., what share of studies has been published and which has not), we examine the prevalence of unpublished research on both the study and the hypothesis level (e.g. which hypotheses are published and which remain unpublished). We did so by comparing the submissions of 178 studies conducted in two probability-based German panel infrastructures (SOEP-IS and GESIS Panel) with their subsequent publications. We found substantial evidence for publication bias: after an average of more than six years of data availability, 43.8% of studies remained unpublished, 44.4% published at least one peer-reviewed article, and 11.8% published gray literature exclusively. Less than one third of submitted hypotheses were published, while the vast majority of published studies with hypotheses (>95%) included newly added (ad-hoc) hypotheses, and more than half of these studies published exclusively ad-hoc hypotheses. Taken together, over 80% of all published hypotheses were ad-hoc, and over 70% of hypotheses were supported. These findings highlight persistent incentives toward selective reporting and ad-hoc hypothesis formulation (potential HARKing). Our results raise concerns about ongoing publication bias in the social, behavioral, and economic sciences.

articles.title: Methodological Innovations for Measuring Multifaceted Emotions in Context
articles.authors: Sally Hang; Paul Hastings; Camelia Hostinar
articles.abstract: Measuring physiological activity as an index of emotional responses in developing samples has been an ongoing endeavor for decades. However, extant research has not identified physiological patterns that could reliably serve as signatures for emotional experiences outside controlled laboratory environments. Examining the psychophysiology of emotion dynamically and across multiple contexts may provide novel insights into within-person change processes and between-person individual differences that forecast both typical and atypical emotional development outcomes. This chapter guides the integration of several recent technological and computational advancements to study developmental affective psychophysiology in context. Specifically, we discuss emerging research using novel technology (e.g., wearable devices) and computational techniques (e.g., machine learning) to capture and statistically model emotional responses in various contexts, which could aid in creating a multifaceted understanding of emotion dynamics. We propose that assessing the psychophysiology of emotions in developing samples can be enhanced by studying emotions using these new tools in environments outside the laboratory due to the

articles.title: Making Open Science Multilingual: Lessons from the Turkish Adaptation of FORRT’s Open Science Glossary
articles.authors: Elif Bastan; Ege Emek; Merve Öztürk; Neslihan Özhan; Nihan Selin Soylu-Konak; Flavio Azevedo
articles.abstract: In response to the replication crisis, a significant number of structural and procedural changes have been made in how scientific research is conducted. Of these changes, open science practices, in particular, have been seen as a panacea for improving transparency, reproducibility, and accessibility in research. However, engaging in such reforms is not equally possible for everyone in academia, partly due to dominant resources being available in the English language. This situation results in a linguistic power asymmetry that creates and perpetuates structural barriers for researchers, educators, and students working in non-English contexts, overall limiting their efforts to take part in global scientific discourse. Using the frameworks of open science, linguistics, and epistemic justice as an organizing point, this article reports and reflects on translating the Open Science Glossary of the Framework for Open and Reproducible Research Training (FORRT) into Turkish, conducted as part of a multilingual initiative. Our team of five researchers carried out a collaborative translation process and translated approximately 250 open science terms. Translation decisions, particularly for terms without established Turkish equivalents, were debated collectively, and final decisions were made by considering the principles of clarity, accuracy, accessibility, and adaptation. We argue that translating terminology is an act of epistemic inclusion that supports capacity building, teaching, and research reform, rather than simply being a technical necessity. By localising open science concepts in the Turkish language, our work contributes to more inclusive and globally representative open science efforts.

articles.title: Category-Specific Sensitivity Allows Modeling Variability Effects in a Similarity Framework
articles.authors: Florian Ismael Seitz; Rebecca Albrecht
articles.abstract: Categorization is a core cognitive ability that enables humans to treat distinct entities as equivalent members of the same concept. Similarity-based models have been highly successful in explaining a wide range of categorization phenomena, but struggle to account for the category variability effect—the tendency to assign ambiguous items to more variable categories—because they do not explicitly represent distributional properties such as variability. We argue that explaining this effect does not require abandoning similarity-based models, but rather relaxing the assumption that model parameters are fixed across categories. Specifically, we consider two parametric extensions: allowing a bias parameter to favor high-variability categories, and allowing a sensitivity parameter to vary with each category's distributional precision. We directly compare both mechanisms in a model simulation across prototype and exemplar models, evaluating their categorization performance. Results consistently favor category-specific sensitivity over response bias, particularly for prototype models. We interpret this finding in terms of the broader cognitive science literature and extend the sensitivity account to prevalence-induced concept change, arguing that both variability and prevalence effects may reflect a common underlying mechanism in which sensitivity scales with the degree to which a category is specified. Together, these findings point towards a unified similarity-based account of distributional influences on categorization.

articles.title: Does the Speaker Matter: EEG Evidence on the Influence of  Voice Information on Phoneme Learning in Infancy
articles.authors: Gisela Hanna Govaart; Katerina Chladkova; Antonio Schettino; Claudia Männel
articles.abstract: Infants acquire the sound categories of their native language within the first year of life, despite the lack of invariant acoustic features that define these categories across speakers and contexts. Given infants’ ability to process voice information and their preference for the familiar maternal voice, we investigated whether voice familiarity supports the acquisition of phonemes. Specifically, we tested whether infants learn a difficult native vowel contrast better from a familiar speaker compared to an unfamiliar speaker. During a 5-minute phoneme training, 71 3-month-old German-learning infants were exposed to a difficult native vowel contrast (/ɪ/–/ɛ/), spoken either by a previously familiarized speaker or by an unfamiliar speaker. In the subsequent test phase, measuring the electrophysiological mismatch response, we assessed infants’ ability to differentiate the vowels when spoken by either the training speaker or a novel speaker. Using Bayesian analysis, we found evidence against improved vowel discrimination after training with a familiar speaker. Instead, it was training with an unfamiliar speaker that resulted in facilitated vowel discrimination for a novel speaker at test. Our findings suggest that infants may not necessarily benefit from a familiar speaker during phoneme learning, particularly when the speaker is not a caregiver and thus lacks an emotional connection. Learning from an unfamiliar speaker, however, may enhance generalization across speakers, potentially due to increased attention driven by novelty.

articles.title: The Word-Riddle Paradigm for Studying Cheating in Middle Childhood: Evidence from a Twin Study
articles.authors: Lisanne Schroer; Michelle Achterberg
articles.abstract: While societal moral norms emphasize fairness and rule-following, cheating behaviour is surprisingly common among children. However, due to its social undesirability, cheating behaviour is difficult to study. The present preregistered paper introduces a novel word riddle paradigm to assess cheating behaviour in middle childhood. In a longitudinal twin cohort study, 428 8- to 10-year-olds were presented with unsolvable word riddles and left alone while an answer booklet was placed close by. Half of the children received the explicit instruction not to peek. Cheating tendency (whether children cheated) and cheating frequency (number of cheated words) were assessed. Results showed that 46% of children cheated on this task, and cheaters cheated on more than half of the words on average (54%). Reminding children not to peek decreased cheating frequency, but not cheating tendency. Age was negatively associated with cheating tendency, but not with cheating frequency. No cross-sectional or longitudinal associations were found between cheating behaviour and children’s callous-unemotional traits, empathy and effortful control. Finally, an ACE analysis indicated most of the variance (65%) in cheating behaviour was explained by unique environment of the child and/or measurement error, whereas genetic influences accounted for a smaller proportion of the variance (35%). No shared environmental effects were observed. Results are discussed in light of contextual factors that might shape cheating. Taken together, the findings demonstrate that this paradigm provides a simple yet sensitive tool to assess cheating in middle childhood and suggest that cheating is a frequent behaviour in middle childhood, even under explicit prohibitions.

articles.title: Voice-Familiarity Benefit in Infant Phoneme Perception: Acoustic versus Exemplar-based Familiarity
articles.authors: Gisela Hanna Govaart; Katerina Chladkova; Antonio Schettino; Claudia Männel
articles.abstract: Starting in utero, infants perceive voice information and preferentially attend to the maternal voice. In adults, speaker familiarity facilitates word and phoneme perception. The current study investigated whether a similar voice-familiarity benefit supports phoneme perception in early infancy, and whether this benefit is driven by acoustic familiarity with speaker voice, prior exposure to phoneme exemplars, or their combination. We tested 72 German-learning 3-month-olds, who were familiarized at home with two speakers. In the lab, infants received 5 minutes of training on a difficult native vowel contrast (/ɛ/–/ɪ/) from either one of the familiarized speakers or an unfamiliar speaker. Discrimination was tested with the electrophysiological mismatch response using three talkers: (1) the phoneme training speaker (familiarized or unfamiliar, depending on group), (2) the familiarized non-training speaker, and (3) a novel speaker (i.e., an unfamiliarized non-training speaker). This design dissociated acoustic familiarity (at-home familiarization) from phoneme-exemplar exposure (training tokens). Preregistered contrasts provided evidence against discrimination differences across test speakers when training was delivered by the unfamiliar speaker; this training might promote speaker-independent generalization, as predicted by abstractionist accounts of speech perception. By contrast, exploratory analyses revealed a voice-familiarity benefit in the group trained by the familiar speaker; this training may drive infants to rely more strongly on speaker-specific cues. Thus, the voice-familiarity benefit only arose when acoustic familiarity and exemplar-based familiarity were combined, suggesting that neither form alone was sufficient. Together, these results are consistent with hybrid accounts of speech perception in which exemplar-like and abstract representations coexist.

articles.title: Breaking barriers to accessing mental healthcare for autistic people: the Autistic SPACE Framework for adult mental health settings
articles.authors: Catherine Dakin; Daniel Davies; Bernadette Grosjean; Malcolm Kinnear; Jason Lang; Meng-Chuan Lai; Sam Porter; Anna Rebowska; Esther Sabel; Khurram Sadiq
articles.abstract: Autistic adults are at substantially increased risk of major mental disorders and suicide, yet face persistent barriers to timely, appropriate, and affirming mental healthcare. To date, no organised, practice-oriented framework has guided how mental health services should accommodate autistic people. Here, a team of clinicians and academics, including openly autistic clinicians, applies the Autistic SPACE Framework – Sensory, Predictability, Acceptance, Communication, and Empathy, with attention to Physical, Processing, and Emotional space – to mental healthcare, including (1) outpatient and community care, (2) crisis presentations, and (3) inpatient settings. Accommodations are individual, dynamic, and embedded within autism-informed risk formulation. We argue that refusing admission solely on the basis of autism constitutes disability discrimination, and reframe accommodation challenges as service delivery problems rather than reasons for exclusion. Wider adoption of the framework offers a practical route to safer, more equitable, and more therapeutic mental healthcare for autistic adults.
